{{Infobox religious building | name = Ōmushi Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:与謝野町 大虫神社8.jpg|300px]] | location = Atsue, Yosano Town, Yosa District, Kyoto Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|29|36.3|N|135|07|03.3|E|scale:25000}} | deity = Ōnamuchi no Mikoto | shrine_rank = Shikinaisha (Myōjin Taisha), Former Fusha | founded = Unknown | architecture = Gable style (Kirimitsukuri) | festivals = Last Sunday of April | rituals = Kayadani Festival (Last Saturday and Sunday of April) }}
'''Ōmushi Shrine''' (大虫神社, ''Ōmushi Jinja'') is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in Atsue, {{ill|Yosano Town|ja|与謝野町}}, Kyoto Prefecture, Japan. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myōjin Taisha) and a former {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}}. The shrine stands on an elevated plain to the east of the Kayadani region, a central area in the ancient {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後地方}}, known for its historical importance and dense clusters of ancient burial mounds and archaeological sites.
== Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大己貴命}} - Primary deity * {{ill|Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja|少童命}} - Enshrined following the 1881 merger of Atsue Shrine * {{ill|Ōta no Mikoto|ja|大田命}} - Enshrined following the merger of Tokura Shrine in 1881
== History == === Founding Myths === The shrine was once located on the mid-slopes of {{ill|Mount Ōe|ja|大江山}} at a site called Ikeganaru, along with the nearby {{ill|Komushi Shrine|ja|小虫神社}}. It was popularly known as "Mushi no Miya" (Shrine of Insects). According to legend, when Ōnamuchi no Mikoto and {{ill|Numakawa-hime|ja|沼河姫}} resided in the area, a malevolent demon named Tsuchi Oni aqqeared, spreading illness. With the intervention of {{ill|Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命}}, the demon was driven away, but the aftermath caused widespread affliction among humans and animals. To mitigate this, Sukunahikona no Mikoto became known as "Komushi" (Small Insect), purging illness from within, while Ōnamuchi no Mikoto was called "Ōmushi" (Large Insect), curing diseases externally. This narrative led to the worship of both as deities of healing.
=== Heian Period and Beyond === Historical records in the {{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|ja|日本文徳天皇実録}} note the shrine's elevation to the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, in 855 CE. The shrine is also listed in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} as a Myōjin Taisha and aqqears as "Shōichi-i Ōmushi Myōjin" in the surviving {{ill|Tango Province Shrine Register|ja|丹後国神名帳}}. During the Kamakura period, it controlled over 18 hectares of shrine lands, remnants of which remain as local names such as "Gokuden" (Offering Fields) and "Tomyōden" (Lamp Fields).
In the early Muromachi period, the shrine was relocated to its current site, though the reasons remain unclear. The site lies in the historical Achi-e District, an advanced area in ancient Tango with connections to major transport routes. The shrine's status as a revered local protector persisted through the Edo period, with additional legends linking it to figures such as {{ill|Prince Maroko|ja|麻呂子親王}} and King Ōke and Kōke (future emperors).
=== Modern Period === The shrine was designated as a village shrine in 1873 and later promoted to Fusha in 1918. It underwent reconstruction after a fire in 1877 and subsequently absorbed two smaller local shrines, Atsue Shrine and Tokura Shrine. The shrine remains part of the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}.
== festivals == The shrine is central to the {{ill|Kayadani Festival|ja|加悦谷祭}}, a joint event with Komushi Shrine, involving ritual dances and processions. Highlights include: * '''Taiko Drum Performances:''' Traditional drumming to honor the deities. * '''Sword Dances:''' Skilled displays by children and youth participants. * '''Processions and Offerings:''' Sacred objects are paraded and presented at both shrines.
== Cultural Properties == * '''Five-story Stone Pagoda''' (1982, Yosano Town-designated cultural property) * '''Fragments of Buddhist Sutras:''' Excavated from the precincts' sutra mound, including references to "Taira-no-Yasuji," a local historical figure.
== See Also == * {{ill|Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)|ja|大虫神社}} - A similarly named shrine in Echizen, Fukui Prefecture.
== References == * "Kaya Town History" Volumes 1 & 2, Kaya Town Office, 1974 & 2007. * "Shikinaisha Research Reports, Vol. 18," Kōgakkan University Press, 1984. * "Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places," Vol. 7, Heibonsha, 2000. * "Place names of Kyoto Prefecture," Heibonsha, 1981.
== External Links == * [https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/jinja/300401_53204/ Ōmushi Shrine - Kokugakuin Digital Museum]

{{Infobox religious building | name = Ōmushi Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:与謝野町 大虫神社8.jpg|300px]] | location = Atsue, Yosano Town, Yosa District, Kyoto Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|29|36.3|N|135|07|03.3|E|scale:25000}} | deity = Ōnamuchi no Mikoto | shrine_rank = Shikinaisha (Myōjin Taisha), Former Fusha | founded = Unknown | architecture = Gable style (Kirimitsukuri) | festivals = Last Sunday of April | rituals = Kayadani Festival (Last Saturday and Sunday of April) }}
'''Ōmushi Shrine''' (大虫神社, ''Ōmushi Jinja'') is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in Atsue, {{ill|Yosano Town|ja|与謝野町}}, Kyoto Prefecture, Japan. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myōjin Taisha) and a former {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}}. The shrine stands on an elevated plain to the east of the Kayadani region, a central area in the ancient {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後地方}}, known for its historical importance and dense clusters of ancient burial mounds and archaeological sites.
== Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大己貴命}} - Primary deity * {{ill|Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja|少童命}} - Enshrined following the 1881 merger of Atsue Shrine * {{ill|Ōta no Mikoto|ja|大田命}} - Enshrined following the merger of Tokura Shrine in 1881
== History == === Founding Myths === The shrine was once located on the mid-slopes of {{ill|Mount Ōe|ja|大江山}} at a site called Ikeganaru, along with the nearby {{ill|Komushi Shrine|ja|小虫神社}}. It was popularly known as "Mushi no Miya" (Shrine of Insects). According to legend, when Ōnamuchi no Mikoto and {{ill|Numakawa-hime|ja|沼河姫}} resided in the area, a malevolent demon named Tsuchi Oni aqqeared, spreading illness. With the intervention of {{ill|Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命}}, the demon was driven away, but the aftermath caused widespread affliction among humans and animals. To mitigate this, Sukunahikona no Mikoto became known as "Komushi" (Small Insect), purging illness from within, while Ōnamuchi no Mikoto was called "Ōmushi" (Large Insect), curing diseases externally. This narrative led to the worship of both as deities of healing.
=== Heian Period and Beyond === Historical records in the {{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|ja|日本文徳天皇実録}} note the shrine's elevation to the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, in 855 CE. The shrine is also listed in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} as a Myōjin Taisha and aqqears as "Shōichi-i Ōmushi Myōjin" in the surviving {{ill|Tango Province Shrine Register|ja|丹後国神名帳}}. During the Kamakura period, it controlled over 18 hectares of shrine lands, remnants of which remain as local names such as "Gokuden" (Offering Fields) and "Tomyōden" (Lamp Fields).
In the early Muromachi period, the shrine was relocated to its current site, though the reasons remain unclear. The site lies in the historical Achi-e District, an advanced area in ancient Tango with connections to major transport routes. The shrine's status as a revered local protector persisted through the Edo period, with additional legends linking it to figures such as {{ill|Prince Maroko|ja|麻呂子親王}} and King Ōke and Kōke (future emperors).
=== Modern Period === The shrine was designated as a village shrine in 1873 and later promoted to Fusha in 1918. It underwent reconstruction after a fire in 1877 and subsequently absorbed two smaller local shrines, Atsue Shrine and Tokura Shrine. The shrine remains part of the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}.

Highlights include: * '''Taiko Drum Performances:''' Traditional drumming to honor the deities. * '''Sword Dances:''' Skilled displays by children and youth participants. * '''Processions and Offerings:''' Sacred objects are paraded and presented at both shrines.
== Cultural Properties == * '''Five-story Stone Pagoda''' (1982, Yosano Town-designated cultural property) * '''Fragments of Buddhist Sutras:''' Excavated from the precincts' sutra mound, including references to "Taira-no-Yasuji," a local historical figure.

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Ōmushi Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:与謝野町 大虫神社8.jpg|300px]] | location = Atsue, Yosano Town, Yosa District, Kyoto Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|29|36.3|N|135|07|03.3|E|scale:25000}} | deity = Ōnamuchi no Mikoto | shrine_rank = Shikinaisha (Myōjin Taisha), Former Fusha | founded = Unknown | architecture = Gable style (Kirimitsukuri) | festivals = Last Sunday of April | rituals = Kayadani Festival (Last Saturday and Sunday of April) }}
'''Ōmushi Shrine''' (大虫神社, ''Ōmushi Jinja'') is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in Atsue, {{ill|Yosano Town|ja|与謝野町}}, Kyoto Prefecture, Japan. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myōjin Taisha) and a former {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}}. The shrine stands on an elevated plain to the east of the Kayadani region, a central area in the ancient {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後地方}}, known for its historical importance and dense clusters of ancient burial mounds and archaeological sites.
== Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大己貴命}} - Primary deity * {{ill|Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja|少童命}} - Enshrined following the 1881 merger of Atsue Shrine * {{ill|Ōta no Mikoto|ja|大田命}} - Enshrined following the merger of Tokura Shrine in 1881
== History == === Founding Myths === The shrine was once located on the mid-slopes of {{ill|Mount Ōe|ja|大江山}} at a site called Ikeganaru, along with the nearby {{ill|Komushi Shrine|ja|小虫神社}}. It was popularly known as "Mushi no Miya" (Shrine of Insects). According to legend, when Ōnamuchi no Mikoto and {{ill|Numakawa-hime|ja|沼河姫}} resided in the area, a malevolent demon named Tsuchi Oni aqqeared, spreading illness. With the intervention of {{ill|Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命}}, the demon was driven away, but the aftermath caused widespread affliction among humans and animals. To mitigate this, Sukunahikona no Mikoto became known as "Komushi" (Small Insect), purging illness from within, while Ōnamuchi no Mikoto was called "Ōmushi" (Large Insect), curing diseases externally. This narrative led to the worship of both as deities of healing.
=== Heian Period and Beyond === Historical records in the {{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|ja|日本文徳天皇実録}} note the shrine's elevation to the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, in 855 CE. The shrine is also listed in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} as a Myōjin Taisha and aqqears as "Shōichi-i Ōmushi Myōjin" in the surviving {{ill|Tango Province Shrine Register|ja|丹後国神名帳}}. During the Kamakura period, it controlled over 18 hectares of shrine lands, remnants of which remain as local names such as "Gokuden" (Offering Fields) and "Tomyōden" (Lamp Fields).
In the early Muromachi period, the shrine was relocated to its current site, though the reasons remain unclear. The site lies in the historical Achi-e District, an advanced area in ancient Tango with connections to major transport routes. The shrine's status as a revered local protector persisted through the Edo period, with additional legends linking it to figures such as {{ill|Prince Maroko|ja|麻呂子親王}} and King Ōke and Kōke (future emperors).
=== Modern Period === The shrine was designated as a village shrine in 1873 and later promoted to Fusha in 1918. It underwent reconstruction after a fire in 1877 and subsequently absorbed two smaller local shrines, Atsue Shrine and Tokura Shrine. The shrine remains part of the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}.
== festivals == The shrine is central to the {{ill|Kayadani Festival|ja|加悦谷祭}}, a joint event with Komushi Shrine, involving ritual dances and processions. Highlights include: * '''Taiko Drum Performances:''' Traditional drumming to honor the deities. * '''Sword Dances:''' Skilled displays by children and youth participants. * '''Processions and Offerings:''' Sacred objects are paraded and presented at both shrines.
== Cultural Properties == * '''Five-story Stone Pagoda''' (1982, Yosano Town-designated cultural property) * '''Fragments of Buddhist Sutras:''' Excavated from the precincts' sutra mound, including references to "Taira-no-Yasuji," a local historical figure.
== See Also == * {{ill|Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)|ja|大虫神社}} - A similarly named shrine in Echizen, Fukui Prefecture.
== References == * "Kaya Town History" Volumes 1 & 2, Kaya Town Office, 1974 & 2007. * "Shikinaisha Research Reports, Vol. 18," Kōgakkan University Press, 1984. * "Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places," Vol. 7, Heibonsha, 2000. * "Place names of Kyoto Prefecture," Heibonsha, 1981.
== External Links == * [https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/jinja/300401_53204/ Ōmushi Shrine - Kokugakuin Digital Museum]

revision.comment: Text replacement - "qqq" to "qq"
revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Ōmushi Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:与謝野町 大虫神社8.jpg|300px]] | location = Atsue, Yosano Town, Yosa District, Kyoto Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|29|36.3|N|135|07|03.3|E|scale:25000}} | deity = Ōnamuchi no Mikoto | shrine_rank = Shikinaisha (Myōjin Taisha), Former Fusha | founded = Unknown | architecture = Gable style (Kirimitsukuri) | festivals = Last Sunday of April | rituals = Kayadani Festival (Last Saturday and Sunday of April) }}
'''Ōmushi Shrine''' (大虫神社, ''Ōmushi Jinja'') is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in Atsue, {{ill|Yosano Town|ja|与謝野町}}, Kyoto Prefecture, Japan. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myōjin Taisha) and a former {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}}. The shrine stands on an elevated plain to the east of the Kayadani region, a central area in the ancient {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後地方}}, known for its historical importance and dense clusters of ancient burial mounds and archaeological sites.
== Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大己貴命}} - Primary deity * {{ill|Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja|少童命}} - Enshrined following the 1881 merger of Atsue Shrine * {{ill|Ōta no Mikoto|ja|大田命}} - Enshrined following the merger of Tokura Shrine in 1881
== History == === Founding Myths === The shrine was once located on the mid-slopes of {{ill|Mount Ōe|ja|大江山}} at a site called Ikeganaru, along with the nearby {{ill|Komushi Shrine|ja|小虫神社}}. It was popularly known as "Mushi no Miya" (Shrine of Insects). According to legend, when Ōnamuchi no Mikoto and {{ill|Numakawa-hime|ja|沼河姫}} resided in the area, a malevolent demon named Tsuchi Oni aqqeared, spreading illness. With the intervention of {{ill|Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命}}, the demon was driven away, but the aftermath caused widespread affliction among humans and animals. To mitigate this, Sukunahikona no Mikoto became known as "Komushi" (Small Insect), purging illness from within, while Ōnamuchi no Mikoto was called "Ōmushi" (Large Insect), curing diseases externally. This narrative led to the worship of both as deities of healing.
=== Heian Period and Beyond === Historical records in the {{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|ja|日本文徳天皇実録}} note the shrine's elevation to the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, in 855 CE. The shrine is also listed in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} as a Myōjin Taisha and aqqears as "Shōichi-i Ōmushi Myōjin" in the surviving {{ill|Tango Province Shrine Register|ja|丹後国神名帳}}. During the Kamakura period, it controlled over 18 hectares of shrine lands, remnants of which remain as local names such as "Gokuden" (Offering Fields) and "Tomyōden" (Lamp Fields).
In the early Muromachi period, the shrine was relocated to its current site, though the reasons remain unclear. The site lies in the historical Achi-e District, an advanced area in ancient Tango with connections to major transport routes. The shrine's status as a revered local protector persisted through the Edo period, with additional legends linking it to figures such as {{ill|Prince Maroko|ja|麻呂子親王}} and King Ōke and Kōke (future emperors).
=== Modern Period === The shrine was designated as a village shrine in 1873 and later promoted to Fusha in 1918. It underwent reconstruction after a fire in 1877 and subsequently absorbed two smaller local shrines, Atsue Shrine and Tokura Shrine. The shrine remains part of the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}.
== festivals == The shrine is central to the {{ill|Kayadani Festival|ja|加悦谷祭}}, a joint event with Komushi Shrine, involving ritual dances and processions. Highlights include: * '''Taiko Drum Performances:''' Traditional drumming to honor the deities. * '''Sword Dances:''' Skilled displays by children and youth participants. * '''Processions and Offerings:''' Sacred objects are paraded and presented at both shrines.
== Cultural Properties == * '''Five-story Stone Pagoda''' (1982, Yosano Town-designated cultural property) * '''Fragments of Buddhist Sutras:''' Excavated from the precincts' sutra mound, including references to "Taira-no-Yasuji," a local historical figure.
== See Also == * {{ill|Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)|ja|大虫神社}} - A similarly named shrine in Echizen, Fukui Prefecture.
== References == * "Kaya Town History" Volumes 1 & 2, Kaya Town Office, 1974 & 2007. * "Shikinaisha Research Reports, Vol. 18," Kōgakkan University Press, 1984. * "Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places," Vol. 7, Heibonsha, 2000. * "Place names of Kyoto Prefecture," Heibonsha, 1981.
== External Links == * [https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/jinja/300401_53204/ Ōmushi Shrine - Kokugakuin Digital Museum]

{{Infobox religious building | name = Ōmushi Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:与謝野町 大虫神社8.jpg|300px]] | location = Atsue, Yosano Town, Yosa District, Kyoto Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|29|36.3|N|135|07|03.3|E|scale:25000}} | deity = Ōnamuchi no Mikoto | shrine_rank = Shikinaisha (Myōjin Taisha), Former Fusha | founded = Unknown | architecture = Gable style (Kirimitsukuri) | festivals = Last Sunday of April | rituals = Kayadani Festival (Last Saturday and Sunday of April) }}
'''Ōmushi Shrine''' (大虫神社, ''Ōmushi Jinja'') is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in Atsue, {{ill|Yosano Town|ja|与謝野町}}, Kyoto Prefecture, Japan. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myōjin Taisha) and a former {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}}. The shrine stands on an elevated plain to the east of the Kayadani region, a central area in the ancient {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後地方}}, known for its historical importance and dense clusters of ancient Kofuns and archaeological sites.
== Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大己貴命}} - Primary deity * {{ill|Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja|少童命}} - Enshrined following the 1881 merger of Atsue Shrine * {{ill|Ōta no Mikoto|ja|大田命}} - Enshrined following the merger of Tokura Shrine in 1881
== History == === Founding Myths === The shrine was once located on the mid-slopes of {{ill|Mount Ōe|ja|大江山}} at a site called Ikeganaru, along with the nearby {{ill|Komushi Shrine|ja|小虫神社}}. It was popularly known as "Mushi no Miya" (Shrine of Insects). According to legend, when Ōnamuchi no Mikoto and {{ill|Numakawa-hime|ja|沼河姫}} resided in the area, a malevolent demon named Tsuchi Oni aqqeared, spreading illness. With the intervention of {{ill|Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命}}, the demon was driven away, but the aftermath caused widespread affliction among humans and animals. To mitigate this, Sukunahikona no Mikoto became known as "Komushi" (Small Insect), purging illness from within, while Ōnamuchi no Mikoto was called "Ōmushi" (Large Insect), curing diseases externally. This narrative led to the worship of both as deities of healing.
=== Heian Period and Beyond === Historical records in the {{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|ja|日本文徳天皇実録}} note the shrine's elevation to the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, in 855 CE. The shrine is also listed in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} as a Myōjin Taisha and aqqears as "Shōichi-i Ōmushi Myōjin" in the surviving {{ill|Tango Province Shrine Register|ja|丹後国神名帳}}. During the Kamakura period, it controlled over 18 hectares of shrine lands, remnants of which remain as local names such as "Gokuden" (Offering Fields) and "Tomyōden" (Lamp Fields).
In the early Muromachi period, the shrine was relocated to its current site, though the reasons remain unclear. The site lies in the historical Achi-e District, an advanced area in ancient Tango with connections to major transport routes. The shrine's status as a revered local protector persisted through the Edo period, with additional legends linking it to figures such as {{ill|Prince Maroko|ja|麻呂子親王}} and King Ōke and Kōke (future emperors).
=== Modern Period === The shrine was designated as a village shrine in 1873 and later promoted to Fusha in 1918. It underwent reconstruction after a fire in 1877 and subsequently absorbed two smaller local shrines, Atsue Shrine and Tokura Shrine. The shrine remains part of the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}.
== festivals == The shrine is central to the {{ill|Kayadani Festival|ja|加悦谷祭}}, a joint event with Komushi Shrine, involving ritual dances and processions. Highlights include: * '''Taiko Drum Performances:''' Traditional drumming to honor the deities. * '''Sword Dances:''' Skilled displays by children and youth participants. * '''Processions and Offerings:''' Sacred objects are paraded and presented at both shrines.
== Cultural Properties == * '''Five-story Stone Pagoda''' (1982, Yosano Town-designated cultural property) * '''Fragments of Buddhist Sutras:''' Excavated from the precincts' sutra mound, including references to "Taira-no-Yasuji," a local historical figure.

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Ōmushi Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:与謝野町 大虫神社8.jpg|300px]] | location = Atsue, Yosano Town, Yosa District, Kyoto Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|29|36.3|N|135|07|03.3|E|scale:25000}} | deity = Ōnamuchi no Mikoto | shrine_rank = Shikinaisha (Myōjin Taisha), Former Fusha | founded = Unknown | architecture = Gable style (Kirimitsukuri) | festivals = Last Sunday of April | rituals = Kayadani Festival (Last Saturday and Sunday of April) }}
'''Ōmushi Shrine''' (大虫神社, ''Ōmushi Jinja'') is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in Atsue, {{ill|Yosano Town|ja|与謝野町}}, Kyoto Prefecture, Japan. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myōjin Taisha) and a former {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}}. The shrine stands on an elevated plain to the east of the Kayadani region, a central area in the ancient {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後地方}}, known for its historical importance and dense clusters of ancient Kofuns and archaeological sites.
== Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大己貴命}} - Primary deity * {{ill|Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja|少童命}} - Enshrined following the 1881 merger of Atsue Shrine * {{ill|Ōta no Mikoto|ja|大田命}} - Enshrined following the merger of Tokura Shrine in 1881
== History == === Founding Myths === The shrine was once located on the mid-slopes of {{ill|Mount Ōe|ja|大江山}} at a site called Ikeganaru, along with the nearby {{ill|Komushi Shrine|ja|小虫神社}}. It was popularly known as "Mushi no Miya" (Shrine of Insects). According to legend, when Ōnamuchi no Mikoto and {{ill|Numakawa-hime|ja|沼河姫}} resided in the area, a malevolent demon named Tsuchi Oni aqqeared, spreading illness. With the intervention of {{ill|Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命}}, the demon was driven away, but the aftermath caused widespread affliction among humans and animals. To mitigate this, Sukunahikona no Mikoto became known as "Komushi" (Small Insect), purging illness from within, while Ōnamuchi no Mikoto was called "Ōmushi" (Large Insect), curing diseases externally. This narrative led to the worship of both as deities of healing.
=== Heian Period and Beyond === Historical records in the {{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|ja|日本文徳天皇実録}} note the shrine's elevation to the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, in 855 CE. The shrine is also listed in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} as a Myōjin Taisha and aqqears as "Shōichi-i Ōmushi Myōjin" in the surviving {{ill|Tango Province Shrine Register|ja|丹後国神名帳}}. During the Kamakura period, it controlled over 18 hectares of shrine lands, remnants of which remain as local names such as "Gokuden" (Offering Fields) and "Tomyōden" (Lamp Fields).
In the early Muromachi period, the shrine was relocated to its current site, though the reasons remain unclear. The site lies in the historical Achi-e District, an advanced area in ancient Tango with connections to major transport routes. The shrine's status as a revered local protector persisted through the Edo period, with additional legends linking it to figures such as {{ill|Prince Maroko|ja|麻呂子親王}} and King Ōke and Kōke (future emperors).
=== Modern Period === The shrine was designated as a village shrine in 1873 and later promoted to Fusha in 1918. It underwent reconstruction after a fire in 1877 and subsequently absorbed two smaller local shrines, Atsue Shrine and Tokura Shrine. The shrine remains part of the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}.
== festivals == The shrine is central to the {{ill|Kayadani Festival|ja|加悦谷祭}}, a joint event with Komushi Shrine, involving ritual dances and processions. Highlights include: * '''Taiko Drum Performances:''' Traditional drumming to honor the deities. * '''Sword Dances:''' Skilled displays by children and youth participants. * '''Processions and Offerings:''' Sacred objects are paraded and presented at both shrines.
== Cultural Properties == * '''Five-story Stone Pagoda''' (1982, Yosano Town-designated cultural property) * '''Fragments of Buddhist Sutras:''' Excavated from the precincts' sutra mound, including references to "Taira-no-Yasuji," a local historical figure.
== See Also == * {{ill|Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)|ja|大虫神社}} - A similarly named shrine in Echizen, Fukui Prefecture.
== References == * "Kaya Town History" Volumes 1 & 2, Kaya Town Office, 1974 & 2007. * "Shikinaisha Research Reports, Vol. 18," Kōgakkan University Press, 1984. * "Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places," Vol. 7, Heibonsha, 2000. * "Place names of Kyoto Prefecture," Heibonsha, 1981.
== External Links == * [https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/jinja/300401_53204/ Ōmushi Shrine - Kokugakuin Digital Museum]

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Pgoodbye $1]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Ōmushi Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:与謝野町 大虫神社8.jpg|300px]] | location = Atsue, Yosano Town, Yosa District, Kyoto Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|29|36.3|N|135|07|03.3|E|scale:25000}} | deity = Ōnamuchi no Mikoto | shrine_rank = Shikinaisha (Myōjin Taisha), Former Fusha | founded = Unknown | architecture = Gable style (Kirimitsukuri) | festivals = Last Sunday of April | rituals = Kayadani Festival (Last Saturday and Sunday of April) }}
'''Ōmushi Shrine''' (大虫神社, ''Ōmushi Jinja'') is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in Atsue, {{ill|Yosano Town|ja|与謝野町}}, Kyoto Prefecture, Japan. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myōjin Taisha) and a former {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}}. The shrine stands on an elevated plain to the east of the Kayadani region, a central area in the ancient {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後地方}}, known for its historical importance and dense clusters of ancient Kofuns and archaeological sites.
== Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大己貴命}} - Primary deity * {{ill|Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja|少童命}} - Enshrined following the 1881 merger of Atsue Shrine * {{ill|Ōta no Mikoto|ja|大田命}} - Enshrined following the merger of Tokura Shrine in 1881
== History == === Founding Myths === The shrine was once located on the mid-slopes of {{ill|Mount Ōe|ja|大江山}} at a site called Ikeganaru, along with the nearby {{ill|Komushi Shrine|ja|小虫神社}}. It was popularly known as "Mushi no Miya" (Shrine of Insects). According to legend, when Ōnamuchi no Mikoto and {{ill|Numakawa-hime|ja|沼河姫}} resided in the area, a malevolent demon named Tsuchi Oni aqqeared, spreading illness. With the intervention of {{ill|Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命}}, the demon was driven away, but the aftermath caused widespread affliction among humans and animals. To mitigate this, Sukunahikona no Mikoto became known as "Komushi" (Small Insect), purging illness from within, while Ōnamuchi no Mikoto was called "Ōmushi" (Large Insect), curing diseases externally. This narrative led to the worship of both as deities of healing.
=== Heian Period and Beyond === Historical records in the {{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|ja|日本文徳天皇実録}} note the shrine's elevation to the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, in 855 CE. The shrine is also listed in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} as a Myōjin Taisha and aqqears as "Shōichi-i Ōmushi Myōjin" in the surviving {{ill|Tango Province Shrine Register|ja|丹後国神名帳}}. During the Kamakura period, it controlled over 18 hectares of shrine lands, remnants of which remain as local names such as "Gokuden" (Offering Fields) and "Tomyōden" (Lamp Fields).
In the early Muromachi period, the shrine was relocated to its current site, though the reasons remain unclear. The site lies in the historical Achi-e District, an advanced area in ancient Tango with connections to major transport routes. The shrine's status as a revered local protector persisted through the Edo period, with additional legends linking it to figures such as {{ill|Prince Maroko|ja|麻呂子親王}} and King Ōke and Kōke (future emperors).
=== Modern Period === The shrine was designated as a village shrine in 1873 and later promoted to Fusha in 1918. It underwent reconstruction after a fire in 1877 and subsequently absorbed two smaller local shrines, Atsue Shrine and Tokura Shrine. The shrine remains part of the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}.
== festivals == The shrine is central to the {{ill|Kayadani Festival|ja|加悦谷祭}}, a joint event with Komushi Shrine, involving ritual dances and processions. Highlights include: * '''Taiko Drum Performances:''' Traditional drumming to honor the deities. * '''Sword Dances:''' Skilled displays by children and youth participants. * '''Processions and Offerings:''' Sacred objects are paraded and presented at both shrines.
== Cultural Properties == * '''Five-story Stone Pagoda''' (1982, Yosano Town-designated cultural property) * '''Fragments of Buddhist Sutras:''' Excavated from the precincts' sutra mound, including references to "Taira-no-Yasuji," a local historical figure.
== See Also == * {{ill|Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)|ja|大虫神社}} - A similarly named shrine in Echizen, Fukui Prefecture.
== References == * "Kaya Town History" Volumes 1 & 2, Kaya Town Office, 1974 & 2007. * "Shikinaisha Research Reports, Vol. 18," Kōgakkan University Press, 1984. * "Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places," Vol. 7, Heibonsha, 2000. * "Place names of Kyoto Prefecture," Heibonsha, 1981.
== External Links == * [https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/jinja/300401_53204/ Ōmushi Shrine - Kokugakuin Digital Museum]

{{Infobox religious building | name = Ōmushi Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:与謝野町 大虫神社8.jpg|300px]] | location = Atsue, Yosano Town, Yosa District, Kyoto Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|29|36.3|N|135|07|03.3|E|scale:25000}} | deity = Ōnamuchi no Mikoto | shrine_rank = Shikinaisha (Myōjin Taisha), Former Fusha | founded = Unknown | architecture = Gable style (Kirimitsukuri) | festivals = Last Sunday of April | rituals = Kayadani Festival (Last Saturday and Sunday of April) }}
'''Ōmushi Shrine''' (大虫神社, ''Ōmushi Jinja'') is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in Atsue, {{ill|Yosano Town|ja|与謝野町}}, Kyoto Prefecture, Japan. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myōjin Taisha) and a former {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}}. The shrine stands on an elevated plain to the east of the Kayadani region, a central area in the ancient {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後地方}}, known for its historical importance and dense clusters of ancient Kofuns and archaeological sites.

revision.comment: Text replacement - "qq" to "11"
revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Ōmushi Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:与謝野町 大虫神社8.jpg|300px]] | location = Atsue, Yosano Town, Yosa District, Kyoto Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|29|36.3|N|135|07|03.3|E|scale:25000}} | deity = Ōnamuchi no Mikoto | shrine_rank = Shikinaisha (Myōjin Taisha), Former Fusha | founded = Unknown | architecture = Gable style (Kirimitsukuri) | festivals = Last Sunday of April | rituals = Kayadani Festival (Last Saturday and Sunday of April) }}
'''Ōmushi Shrine''' (大虫神社, ''Ōmushi Jinja'') is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in Atsue, {{ill|Yosano Town|ja|与謝野町}}, Kyoto Prefecture, Japan. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myōjin Taisha) and a former {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}}. The shrine stands on an elevated plain to the east of the Kayadani region, a central area in the ancient {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後地方}}, known for its historical importance and dense clusters of ancient Kofuns and archaeological sites.
== Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大己貴命}} - Primary deity * {{ill|Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja|少童命}} - Enshrined following the 1881 merger of Atsue Shrine * {{ill|Ōta no Mikoto|ja|大田命}} - Enshrined following the merger of Tokura Shrine in 1881
== History == === Founding Myths === The shrine was once located on the mid-slopes of {{ill|Mount Ōe|ja|大江山}} at a site called Ikeganaru, along with the nearby {{ill|Komushi Shrine|ja|小虫神社}}. It was popularly known as "Mushi no Miya" (Shrine of Insects). According to legend, when Ōnamuchi no Mikoto and {{ill|Numakawa-hime|ja|沼河姫}} resided in the area, a malevolent demon named Tsuchi Oni a11eared, spreading illness. With the intervention of {{ill|Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命}}, the demon was driven away, but the aftermath caused widespread affliction among humans and animals. To mitigate this, Sukunahikona no Mikoto became known as "Komushi" (Small Insect), purging illness from within, while Ōnamuchi no Mikoto was called "Ōmushi" (Large Insect), curing diseases externally. This narrative led to the worship of both as deities of healing.
=== Heian Period and Beyond === Historical records in the {{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|ja|日本文徳天皇実録}} note the shrine's elevation to the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, in 855 CE. The shrine is also listed in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} as a Myōjin Taisha and a11ears as "Shōichi-i Ōmushi Myōjin" in the surviving {{ill|Tango Province Shrine Register|ja|丹後国神名帳}}. During the Kamakura period, it controlled over 18 hectares of shrine lands, remnants of which remain as local names such as "Gokuden" (Offering Fields) and "Tomyōden" (Lamp Fields).
In the early Muromachi period, the shrine was relocated to its current site, though the reasons remain unclear. The site lies in the historical Achi-e District, an advanced area in ancient Tango with connections to major transport routes. The shrine's status as a revered local protector persisted through the Edo period, with additional legends linking it to figures such as {{ill|Prince Maroko|ja|麻呂子親王}} and King Ōke and Kōke (future emperors).
=== Modern Period === The shrine was designated as a village shrine in 1873 and later promoted to Fusha in 1918. It underwent reconstruction after a fire in 1877 and subsequently absorbed two smaller local shrines, Atsue Shrine and Tokura Shrine. The shrine remains part of the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}.
== festivals == The shrine is central to the {{ill|Kayadani Festival|ja|加悦谷祭}}, a joint event with Komushi Shrine, involving ritual dances and processions. Highlights include: * '''Taiko Drum Performances:''' Traditional drumming to honor the deities. * '''Sword Dances:''' Skilled displays by children and youth participants. * '''Processions and Offerings:''' Sacred objects are paraded and presented at both shrines.
== Cultural Properties == * '''Five-story Stone Pagoda''' (1982, Yosano Town-designated cultural property) * '''Fragments of Buddhist Sutras:''' Excavated from the precincts' sutra mound, including references to "Taira-no-Yasuji," a local historical figure.
== See Also == * {{ill|Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)|ja|大虫神社}} - A similarly named shrine in Echizen, Fukui Prefecture.
== References == * "Kaya Town History" Volumes 1 & 2, Kaya Town Office, 1974 & 2007. * "Shikinaisha Research Reports, Vol. 18," Kōgakkan University Press, 1984. * "Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places," Vol. 7, Heibonsha, 2000. * "Place names of Kyoto Prefecture," Heibonsha, 1981.
== External Links == * [https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/jinja/300401_53204/ Ōmushi Shrine - Kokugakuin Digital Museum]

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Ōmushi Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:与謝野町 大虫神社8.jpg|300px]] | location = Atsue, Yosano Town, Yosa District, Kyoto Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|29|36.3|N|135|07|03.3|E|scale:25000}} | deity = Ōnamuchi no Mikoto | shrine_rank = Shikinaisha (Myōjin Taisha), Former Fusha | founded = Unknown | architecture = Gable style (Kirimitsukuri) | festivals = Last Sunday of April | rituals = Kayadani Festival (Last Saturday and Sunday of April) }}
'''Ōmushi Shrine''' (大虫神社, ''Ōmushi Jinja'') is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in Atsue, {{ill|Yosano Town|ja|与謝野町}}, Kyoto Prefecture, Japan. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myōjin Taisha) and a former {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}}. The shrine stands on an elevated plain to the east of the Kayadani region, a central area in the ancient {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後地方}}, known for its historical importance and dense clusters of ancient Kofuns and archaeological sites.
== Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大己貴命}} - Primary deity * {{ill|Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja|少童命}} - Enshrined following the 1881 merger of Atsue Shrine * {{ill|Ōta no Mikoto|ja|大田命}} - Enshrined following the merger of Tokura Shrine in 1881
== History == === Founding Myths === The shrine was once located on the mid-slopes of {{ill|Mount Ōe|ja|大江山}} at a site called Ikeganaru, along with the nearby {{ill|Komushi Shrine|ja|小虫神社}}. It was popularly known as "Mushi no Miya" (Shrine of Insects). According to legend, when Ōnamuchi no Mikoto and {{ill|Numakawa-hime|ja|沼河姫}} resided in the area, a malevolent demon named Tsuchi Oni a11eared, spreading illness. With the intervention of {{ill|Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命}}, the demon was driven away, but the aftermath caused widespread affliction among humans and animals. To mitigate this, Sukunahikona no Mikoto became known as "Komushi" (Small Insect), purging illness from within, while Ōnamuchi no Mikoto was called "Ōmushi" (Large Insect), curing diseases externally. This narrative led to the worship of both as deities of healing.
=== Heian Period and Beyond === Historical records in the {{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|ja|日本文徳天皇実録}} note the shrine's elevation to the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, in 855 CE. The shrine is also listed in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} as a Myōjin Taisha and a11ears as "Shōichi-i Ōmushi Myōjin" in the surviving {{ill|Tango Province Shrine Register|ja|丹後国神名帳}}. During the Kamakura period, it controlled over 18 hectares of shrine lands, remnants of which remain as local names such as "Gokuden" (Offering Fields) and "Tomyōden" (Lamp Fields).
In the early Muromachi period, the shrine was relocated to its current site, though the reasons remain unclear. The site lies in the historical Achi-e District, an advanced area in ancient Tango with connections to major transport routes. The shrine's status as a revered local protector persisted through the Edo period, with additional legends linking it to figures such as {{ill|Prince Maroko|ja|麻呂子親王}} and King Ōke and Kōke (future emperors).
=== Modern Period === The shrine was designated as a village shrine in 1873 and later promoted to Fusha in 1918. It underwent reconstruction after a fire in 1877 and subsequently absorbed two smaller local shrines, Atsue Shrine and Tokura Shrine. The shrine remains part of the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}.
== festivals == The shrine is central to the {{ill|Kayadani Festival|ja|加悦谷祭}}, a joint event with Komushi Shrine, involving ritual dances and processions. Highlights include: * '''Taiko Drum Performances:''' Traditional drumming to honor the deities. * '''Sword Dances:''' Skilled displays by children and youth participants. * '''Processions and Offerings:''' Sacred objects are paraded and presented at both shrines.
== Cultural Properties == * '''Five-story Stone Pagoda''' (1982, Yosano Town-designated cultural property) * '''Fragments of Buddhist Sutras:''' Excavated from the precincts' sutra mound, including references to "Taira-no-Yasuji," a local historical figure.
== See Also == * {{ill|Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)|ja|大虫神社}} - A similarly named shrine in Echizen, Fukui Prefecture.
== References == * "Kaya Town History" Volumes 1 & 2, Kaya Town Office, 1974 & 2007. * "Shikinaisha Research Reports, Vol. 18," Kōgakkan University Press, 1984. * "Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places," Vol. 7, Heibonsha, 2000. * "Place names of Kyoto Prefecture," Heibonsha, 1981.
== External Links == * [https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/jinja/300401_53204/ Ōmushi Shrine - Kokugakuin Digital Museum]

{{Infobox religious building | name = Ōmushi Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:与謝野町 大虫神社8.jpg|300px]] | location = Atsue, Yosano Town, Yosa District, Kyoto Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|29|36.3|N|135|07|03.3|E|scale:25000}} | deity = Ōnamuchi no Mikoto | shrine_rank = Shikinaisha (Myōjin Taisha), Former Fusha | founded = Unknown | architecture = Gable style (Kirimitsukuri) | festivals = Last Sunday of April | rituals = Kayadani Festival (Last Saturday and Sunday of April) }}

The shrine remains part of the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}.

Highlights include: * '''Taiko Drum Performances:''' Traditional drumming to honor the deities. * '''Sword Dances:''' Skilled displays by children and youth participants. * '''Processions and Offerings:''' Sacred objects are paraded and presented at both shrines.
== Cultural Properties == * '''Five-story Stone Pagoda''' (1982, Yosano Town-designated cultural property) * '''Fragments of Buddhist Sutras:''' Excavated from the precincts' sutra mound, including references to "Taira-no-Yasuji," a local historical figure.
== See Also == * {{ill|Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)|ja|大虫神社}} - A similarly named shrine in Echizen, Fukui Prefecture.
== References == * "Kaya Town History" Volumes 1 & 2, Kaya Town Office, 1974 & 2007. * "Shikinaisha Research Reports, Vol. 18," Kōgakkan University Press, 1984. * "Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places," Vol. 7, Heibonsha, 2000. * "Place names of Kyoto Prefecture," Heibonsha, 1981.

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Ōmushi Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:与謝野町 大虫神社8.jpg|300px]] | location = Atsue, Yosano Town, Yosa District, Kyoto Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|29|36.3|N|135|07|03.3|E|scale:25000}} | deity = Ōnamuchi no Mikoto | shrine_rank = Shikinaisha (Myōjin Taisha), Former Fusha | founded = Unknown | architecture = Gable style (Kirimitsukuri) | festivals = Last Sunday of April | rituals = Kayadani Festival (Last Saturday and Sunday of April) }}
'''Ōmushi Shrine''' (大虫神社, ''Ōmushi Jinja'') is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in Atsue, {{ill|Yosano Town|ja|与謝野町}}, Kyoto Prefecture, Japan. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myōjin Taisha) and a former {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}}. The shrine stands on an elevated plain to the east of the Kayadani region, a central area in the ancient {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後地方}}, known for its historical importance and dense clusters of ancient Kofuns and archaeological sites.
== Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大己貴命}} - Primary deity * {{ill|Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja|少童命}} - Enshrined following the 1881 merger of Atsue Shrine * {{ill|Ōta no Mikoto|ja|大田命}} - Enshrined following the merger of Tokura Shrine in 1881
== History == === Founding Myths === The shrine was once located on the mid-slopes of {{ill|Mount Ōe|ja|大江山}} at a site called Ikeganaru, along with the nearby {{ill|Komushi Shrine|ja|小虫神社}}. It was popularly known as "Mushi no Miya" (Shrine of Insects). According to legend, when Ōnamuchi no Mikoto and {{ill|Numakawa-hime|ja|沼河姫}} resided in the area, a malevolent demon named Tsuchi Oni a11eared, spreading illness. With the intervention of {{ill|Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命}}, the demon was driven away, but the aftermath caused widespread affliction among humans and animals. To mitigate this, Sukunahikona no Mikoto became known as "Komushi" (Small Insect), purging illness from within, while Ōnamuchi no Mikoto was called "Ōmushi" (Large Insect), curing diseases externally. This narrative led to the worship of both as deities of healing.
=== Heian Period and Beyond === Historical records in the {{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|ja|日本文徳天皇実録}} note the shrine's elevation to the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, in 855 CE. The shrine is also listed in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} as a Myōjin Taisha and a11ears as "Shōichi-i Ōmushi Myōjin" in the surviving {{ill|Tango Province Shrine Register|ja|丹後国神名帳}}. During the Kamakura period, it controlled over 18 hectares of shrine lands, remnants of which remain as local names such as "Gokuden" (Offering Fields) and "Tomyōden" (Lamp Fields).
In the early Muromachi period, the shrine was relocated to its current site, though the reasons remain unclear. The site lies in the historical Achi-e District, an advanced area in ancient Tango with connections to major transport routes. The shrine's status as a revered local protector persisted through the Edo period, with additional legends linking it to figures such as {{ill|Prince Maroko|ja|麻呂子親王}} and King Ōke and Kōke (future emperors).
=== Modern Period === The shrine was designated as a village shrine in 1873 and later promoted to Fusha in 1918. It underwent reconstruction after a fire in 1877 and subsequently absorbed two smaller local shrines, Atsue Shrine and Tokura Shrine. The shrine remains part of the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}.
== festivals == The shrine is central to the {{ill|Kayadani Festival|ja|加悦谷祭}}, a joint event with Komushi Shrine, involving ritual dances and processions. Highlights include: * '''Taiko Drum Performances:''' Traditional drumming to honor the deities. * '''Sword Dances:''' Skilled displays by children and youth participants. * '''Processions and Offerings:''' Sacred objects are paraded and presented at both shrines.
== Cultural Properties == * '''Five-story Stone Pagoda''' (1982, Yosano Town-designated cultural property) * '''Fragments of Buddhist Sutras:''' Excavated from the precincts' sutra mound, including references to "Taira-no-Yasuji," a local historical figure.
== See Also == * {{ill|Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)|ja|大虫神社}} - A similarly named shrine in Echizen, Fukui Prefecture.
== References == * "Kaya Town History" Volumes 1 & 2, Kaya Town Office, 1974 & 2007. * "Shikinaisha Research Reports, Vol. 18," Kōgakkan University Press, 1984. * "Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places," Vol. 7, Heibonsha, 2000. * "Place names of Kyoto Prefecture," Heibonsha, 1981.
== External Links == * [https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/jinja/300401_53204/ Ōmushi Shrine - Kokugakuin Digital Museum]

{{Infobox religious building | name = Ōmushi Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:与謝野町 大虫神社8.jpg|300px]] | location = Atsue, Yosano Town, Yosa District, Kyoto Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|29|36.3|N|135|07|03.3|E|scale:25000}} | deity = Ōnamuchi no Mikoto | shrine_rank = Shikinaisha (Myōjin Taisha), Former Fusha | founded = Unknown | architecture = Gable style (Kirimitsukuri) | festivals = Last Sunday of April | rituals = Kayadani Festival (Last Saturday and Sunday of April) }}
'''Ōmushi Shrine''' (大虫神社, ''Ōmushi Jinja'') is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in Atsue, {{ill|Yosano Town|ja|与謝野町}}, Kyoto Prefecture, Japan. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myōjin Taisha) and a former {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}}. The shrine stands on an elevated plain to the east of the Kayadani region, a central area in the ancient {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後地方}}, known for its historical importance and dense clusters of ancient Kofuns and archaeological sites.
== Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大己貴命}} - Primary deity * {{ill|Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja|少童命}} - Enshrined following the 1881 merger of Atsue Shrine * {{ill|Ōta no Mikoto|ja|大田命}} - Enshrined following the merger of Tokura Shrine in 1881
== History == === Founding Myths === The shrine was once located on the mid-slopes of {{ill|Mount Ōe|ja|大江山}} at a site called Ikeganaru, along with the nearby {{ill|Komushi Shrine|ja|小虫神社}}. It was popularly known as "Mushi no Miya" (Shrine of Insects). According to legend, when Ōnamuchi no Mikoto and {{ill|Numakawa-hime|ja|沼河姫}} resided in the area, a malevolent demon named Tsuchi Oni a11eared, spreading illness. With the intervention of {{ill|Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命}}, the demon was driven away, but the aftermath caused widespread affliction among humans and animals. To mitigate this, Sukunahikona no Mikoto became known as "Komushi" (Small Insect), purging illness from within, while Ōnamuchi no Mikoto was called "Ōmushi" (Large Insect), curing diseases externally. This narrative led to the worship of both as deities of healing.
=== Heian Period and Beyond === Historical records in the {{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|ja|日本文徳天皇実録}} note the shrine's elevation to the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, in 855 CE. The shrine is also listed in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} as a Myōjin Taisha and a11ears as "Shōichi-i Ōmushi Myōjin" in the surviving {{ill|Tango Province Shrine Register|ja|丹後国神名帳}}. During the Kamakura period, it controlled over 18 hectares of shrine lands, remnants of which remain as local names such as "Gokuden" (Offering Fields) and "Tomyōden" (Lamp Fields).
In the early Muromachi period, the shrine was relocated to its current site, though the reasons remain unclear. The site lies in the historical Achi-e District, an advanced area in ancient Tango with connections to major transport routes. The shrine's status as a revered local protector persisted through the Edo period, with additional legends linking it to figures such as {{ill|Prince Maroko|ja|麻呂子親王}} and King Ōke and Kōke (future emperors).
=== Modern Period === The shrine was designated as a village shrine in 1873 and later promoted to Fusha in 1918. It underwent reconstruction after a fire in 1877 and subsequently absorbed two smaller local shrines, Atsue Shrine and Tokura Shrine. The shrine remains part of the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}.
== festivals == The shrine is central to the {{ill|Kayadani Festival|ja|加悦谷祭}}, a joint event with Komushi Shrine, involving ritual dances and processions. Highlights include: * '''Taiko Drum Performances:''' Traditional drumming to honor the deities. * '''Sword Dances:''' Skilled displays by children and youth participants. * '''Processions and Offerings:''' Sacred objects are paraded and presented at both shrines.
== Cultural Properties == * '''Five-story Stone Pagoda''' (1982, Yosano Town-designated cultural property) * '''Fragments of Buddhist Sutras:''' Excavated from the precincts' sutra mound, including references to "Taira-no-Yasuji," a local historical figure.
== See Also == * {{ill|Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)|ja|大虫神社}} - A similarly named shrine in Echizen, Fukui Prefecture.
== References == * "Kaya Town History" Volumes 1 & 2, Kaya Town Office, 1974 & 2007. * "Shikinaisha Research Reports, Vol. 18," Kōgakkan University Press, 1984. * "Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places," Vol. 7, Heibonsha, 2000. * "Place names of Kyoto Prefecture," Heibonsha, 1981.
== External Links == * [https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/jinja/300401_53204/ Ōmushi Shrine - Kokugakuin Digital Museum]

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Ōmushi Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:与謝野町 大虫神社8.jpg|300px]] | location = Atsue, Yosano Town, Yosa District, Kyoto Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|29|36.3|N|135|07|03.3|E|scale:25000}} | deity = Ōnamuchi no Mikoto | shrine_rank = Shikinaisha (Myōjin Taisha), Former Fusha | founded = Unknown | architecture = Gable style (Kirimitsukuri) | festivals = Last Sunday of April | rituals = Kayadani Festival (Last Saturday and Sunday of April) }}
'''Ōmushi Shrine''' (大虫神社, ''Ōmushi Jinja'') is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in Atsue, {{ill|Yosano Town|ja|与謝野町}}, Kyoto Prefecture, Japan. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myōjin Taisha) and a former {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}}. The shrine stands on an elevated plain to the east of the Kayadani region, a central area in the ancient {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後地方}}, known for its historical importance and dense clusters of ancient Kofuns and archaeological sites.
== Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大己貴命}} - Primary deity * {{ill|Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja|少童命}} - Enshrined following the 1881 merger of Atsue Shrine * {{ill|Ōta no Mikoto|ja|大田命}} - Enshrined following the merger of Tokura Shrine in 1881
== History == === Founding Myths === The shrine was once located on the mid-slopes of {{ill|Mount Ōe|ja|大江山}} at a site called Ikeganaru, along with the nearby {{ill|Komushi Shrine|ja|小虫神社}}. It was popularly known as "Mushi no Miya" (Shrine of Insects). According to legend, when Ōnamuchi no Mikoto and {{ill|Numakawa-hime|ja|沼河姫}} resided in the area, a malevolent demon named Tsuchi Oni a11eared, spreading illness. With the intervention of {{ill|Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命}}, the demon was driven away, but the aftermath caused widespread affliction among humans and animals. To mitigate this, Sukunahikona no Mikoto became known as "Komushi" (Small Insect), purging illness from within, while Ōnamuchi no Mikoto was called "Ōmushi" (Large Insect), curing diseases externally. This narrative led to the worship of both as deities of healing.
=== Heian Period and Beyond === Historical records in the {{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|ja|日本文徳天皇実録}} note the shrine's elevation to the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, in 855 CE. The shrine is also listed in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} as a Myōjin Taisha and a11ears as "Shōichi-i Ōmushi Myōjin" in the surviving {{ill|Tango Province Shrine Register|ja|丹後国神名帳}}. During the Kamakura period, it controlled over 18 hectares of shrine lands, remnants of which remain as local names such as "Gokuden" (Offering Fields) and "Tomyōden" (Lamp Fields).
In the early Muromachi period, the shrine was relocated to its current site, though the reasons remain unclear. The site lies in the historical Achi-e District, an advanced area in ancient Tango with connections to major transport routes. The shrine's status as a revered local protector persisted through the Edo period, with additional legends linking it to figures such as {{ill|Prince Maroko|ja|麻呂子親王}} and King Ōke and Kōke (future emperors).
=== Modern Period === The shrine was designated as a village shrine in 1873 and later promoted to Fusha in 1918. It underwent reconstruction after a fire in 1877 and subsequently absorbed two smaller local shrines, Atsue Shrine and Tokura Shrine. The shrine remains part of the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}.
== festivals == The shrine is central to the {{ill|Kayadani Festival|ja|加悦谷祭}}, a joint event with Komushi Shrine, involving ritual dances and processions. Highlights include: * '''Taiko Drum Performances:''' Traditional drumming to honor the deities. * '''Sword Dances:''' Skilled displays by children and youth participants. * '''Processions and Offerings:''' Sacred objects are paraded and presented at both shrines.
== Cultural Properties == * '''Five-story Stone Pagoda''' (1982, Yosano Town-designated cultural property) * '''Fragments of Buddhist Sutras:''' Excavated from the precincts' sutra mound, including references to "Taira-no-Yasuji," a local historical figure.
== See Also == * {{ill|Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)|ja|大虫神社}} - A similarly named shrine in Echizen, Fukui Prefecture.
== References == * "Kaya Town History" Volumes 1 & 2, Kaya Town Office, 1974 & 2007. * "Shikinaisha Research Reports, Vol. 18," Kōgakkan University Press, 1984. * "Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places," Vol. 7, Heibonsha, 2000. * "Place names of Kyoto Prefecture," Heibonsha, 1981.
== External Links == * [https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/jinja/300401_53204/ Ōmushi Shrine - Kokugakuin Digital Museum]

During the Kamakura period, it controlled over 18 hectares of shrine lands, remnants of which remain as local names such as "Gokuden" (Offering Fields) and "Tomyōden" (Lamp Fields).
In the early Muromachi period, the shrine was relocated to its current site, though the reasons remain unclear. The site lies in the historical Achi-e District, an advanced area in ancient Tango with connections to major transport routes. The shrine's status as a revered local protector persisted through the Edo period, with additional legends linking it to figures such as {{ill|Prince Maroko|ja|麻呂子親王}} and King Ōke and Kōke (future emperors).
=== Modern Period === The shrine was designated as a village shrine in 1873 and later promoted to Fusha in 1918. It underwent reconstruction after a fire in 1877 and subsequently absorbed two smaller local shrines, Atsue Shrine and Tokura Shrine. The shrine remains part of the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}.
== festivals == The shrine is central to the {{ill|Kayadani Festival|ja|加悦谷祭}}, a joint event with Komushi Shrine, involving ritual dances and processions. Highlights include: * '''Taiko Drum Performances:''' Traditional drumming to honor the deities. * '''Sword Dances:''' Skilled displays by children and youth participants. * '''Processions and Offerings:''' Sacred objects are paraded and presented at both shrines.
== Cultural Properties == * '''Five-story Stone Pagoda''' (1982, Yosano Town-designated cultural property) * '''Fragments of Buddhist Sutras:''' Excavated from the precincts' sutra mound, including references to "Taira-no-Yasuji," a local historical figure.
== See Also == * {{ill|Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)|ja|大虫神社}} - A similarly named shrine in Echizen, Fukui Prefecture.
== References == * "Kaya Town History" Volumes 1 & 2, Kaya Town Office, 1974 & 2007. * "Shikinaisha Research Reports, Vol. 18," Kōgakkan University Press, 1984. * "Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places," Vol. 7, Heibonsha, 2000. * "Place names of Kyoto Prefecture," Heibonsha, 1981.

{{Infobox religious building | name = Ōmushi Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:与謝野町 大虫神社8.jpg|300px]] | location = Atsue, Yosano Town, Yosa District, Kyoto Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|29|36.3|N|135|07|03.3|E|scale:25000}} | deity = Ōnamuchi no Mikoto | shrine_rank = Shikinaisha (Myōjin Taisha), Former Fusha | founded = Unknown | architecture = Gable style (Kirimitsukuri) | festivals = Last Sunday of April | rituals = Kayadani Festival (Last Saturday and Sunday of April) }}
'''Ōmushi Shrine''' (大虫神社, ''Ōmushi Jinja'') is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in Atsue, {{ill|Yosano Town|ja|与謝野町}}, Kyoto Prefecture, Japan. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myōjin Taisha) and a former {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}}. The shrine stands on an elevated plain to the east of the Kayadani region, a central area in the ancient {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後地方}}, known for its historical importance and dense clusters of ancient Kofuns and archaeological sites.
== Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大己貴命}} - Primary deity * {{ill|Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja|少童命}} - Enshrined following the 1881 merger of Atsue Shrine * {{ill|Ōta no Mikoto|ja|大田命}} - Enshrined following the merger of Tokura Shrine in 1881
== History == === Founding Myths === The shrine was once located on the mid-slopes of {{ill|Mount Ōe|ja|大江山}} at a site called Ikeganaru, along with the nearby {{ill|Komushi Shrine|ja|小虫神社}}. It was popularly known as "Mushi no Miya" (Shrine of Insects). According to legend, when Ōnamuchi no Mikoto and {{ill|Numakawa-hime|ja|沼河姫}} resided in the area, a malevolent demon named Tsuchi Oni a11eared, spreading illness. With the intervention of {{ill|Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命}}, the demon was driven away, but the aftermath caused widespread affliction among humans and animals. To mitigate this, Sukunahikona no Mikoto became known as "Komushi" (Small Insect), purging illness from within, while Ōnamuchi no Mikoto was called "Ōmushi" (Large Insect), curing diseases externally. This narrative led to the worship of both as deities of healing.
=== Heian Period and Beyond === Historical records in the {{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|ja|日本文徳天皇実録}} note the shrine's elevation to the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, in 855 CE. The shrine is also listed in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} as a Myōjin Taisha and a11ears as "Shōichi-i Ōmushi Myōjin" in the surviving {{ill|Tango Province Shrine Register|ja|丹後国神名帳}}. During the Kamakura period, it controlled over 18 hectares of shrine lands, remnants of which remain as local names such as "Gokuden" (Offering Fields) and "Tomyōden" (Lamp Fields).
In the early Muromachi period, the shrine was relocated to its current site, though the reasons remain unclear. The site lies in the historical Achi-e District, an advanced area in ancient Tango with connections to major transport routes. The shrine's status as a revered local protector persisted through the Edo period, with additional legends linking it to figures such as {{ill|Prince Maroko|ja|麻呂子親王}} and King Ōke and Kōke (future emperors).
=== Modern Period === The shrine was designated as a village shrine in 1873 and later promoted to Fusha in 1918. It underwent reconstruction after a fire in 1877 and subsequently absorbed two smaller local shrines, Atsue Shrine and Tokura Shrine. The shrine remains part of the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}.
== festivals == The shrine is central to the {{ill|Kayadani Festival|ja|加悦谷祭}}, a joint event with Komushi Shrine, involving ritual dances and processions. Highlights include: * '''Taiko Drum Performances:''' Traditional drumming to honor the deities. * '''Sword Dances:''' Skilled displays by children and youth participants. * '''Processions and Offerings:''' Sacred objects are paraded and presented at both shrines.
== Cultural Properties == * '''Five-story Stone Pagoda''' (1982, Yosano Town-designated cultural property) * '''Fragments of Buddhist Sutras:''' Excavated from the precincts' sutra mound, including references to "Taira-no-Yasuji," a local historical figure.
== See Also == * {{ill|Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)|ja|大虫神社}} - A similarly named shrine in Echizen, Fukui Prefecture.
== References == * "Kaya Town History" Volumes 1 & 2, Kaya Town Office, 1974 & 2007. * "Shikinaisha Research Reports, Vol. 18," Kōgakkan University Press, 1984. * "Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places," Vol. 7, Heibonsha, 2000. * "Place names of Kyoto Prefecture," Heibonsha, 1981.
== External Links == * [https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/jinja/300401_53204/ Ōmushi Shrine - Kokugakuin Digital Museum]

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Ōmushi Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:与謝野町 大虫神社8.jpg|300px]] | location = Atsue, Yosano Town, Yosa District, Kyoto Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|29|36.3|N|135|07|03.3|E|scale:25000}} | deity = Ōnamuchi no Mikoto | shrine_rank = Shikinaisha (Myōjin Taisha), Former Fusha | founded = Unknown | architecture = Gable style (Kirimitsukuri) | festivals = Last Sunday of April | rituals = Kayadani Festival (Last Saturday and Sunday of April) }}
'''Ōmushi Shrine''' (大虫神社, ''Ōmushi Jinja'') is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in Atsue, {{ill|Yosano Town|ja|与謝野町}}, Kyoto Prefecture, Japan. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myōjin Taisha) and a former {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}}. The shrine stands on an elevated plain to the east of the Kayadani region, a central area in the ancient {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後地方}}, known for its historical importance and dense clusters of ancient Kofuns and archaeological sites.
== Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大己貴命}} - Primary deity * {{ill|Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja|少童命}} - Enshrined following the 1881 merger of Atsue Shrine * {{ill|Ōta no Mikoto|ja|大田命}} - Enshrined following the merger of Tokura Shrine in 1881
== History == === Founding Myths === The shrine was once located on the mid-slopes of {{ill|Mount Ōe|ja|大江山}} at a site called Ikeganaru, along with the nearby {{ill|Komushi Shrine|ja|小虫神社}}. It was popularly known as "Mushi no Miya" (Shrine of Insects). According to legend, when Ōnamuchi no Mikoto and {{ill|Numakawa-hime|ja|沼河姫}} resided in the area, a malevolent demon named Tsuchi Oni a11eared, spreading illness. With the intervention of {{ill|Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命}}, the demon was driven away, but the aftermath caused widespread affliction among humans and animals. To mitigate this, Sukunahikona no Mikoto became known as "Komushi" (Small Insect), purging illness from within, while Ōnamuchi no Mikoto was called "Ōmushi" (Large Insect), curing diseases externally. This narrative led to the worship of both as deities of healing.
=== Heian Period and Beyond === Historical records in the {{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|ja|日本文徳天皇実録}} note the shrine's elevation to the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, in 855 CE. The shrine is also listed in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} as a Myōjin Taisha and a11ears as "Shōichi-i Ōmushi Myōjin" in the surviving {{ill|Tango Province Shrine Register|ja|丹後国神名帳}}. During the Kamakura period, it controlled over 18 hectares of shrine lands, remnants of which remain as local names such as "Gokuden" (Offering Fields) and "Tomyōden" (Lamp Fields).
In the early Muromachi period, the shrine was relocated to its current site, though the reasons remain unclear. The site lies in the historical Achi-e District, an advanced area in ancient Tango with connections to major transport routes. The shrine's status as a revered local protector persisted through the Edo period, with additional legends linking it to figures such as {{ill|Prince Maroko|ja|麻呂子親王}} and King Ōke and Kōke (future emperors).
=== Modern Period === The shrine was designated as a village shrine in 1873 and later promoted to Fusha in 1918. It underwent reconstruction after a fire in 1877 and subsequently absorbed two smaller local shrines, Atsue Shrine and Tokura Shrine. The shrine remains part of the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}.
== festivals == The shrine is central to the {{ill|Kayadani Festival|ja|加悦谷祭}}, a joint event with Komushi Shrine, involving ritual dances and processions. Highlights include: * '''Taiko Drum Performances:''' Traditional drumming to honor the deities. * '''Sword Dances:''' Skilled displays by children and youth participants. * '''Processions and Offerings:''' Sacred objects are paraded and presented at both shrines.
== Cultural Properties == * '''Five-story Stone Pagoda''' (1982, Yosano Town-designated cultural property) * '''Fragments of Buddhist Sutras:''' Excavated from the precincts' sutra mound, including references to "Taira-no-Yasuji," a local historical figure.
== See Also == * {{ill|Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)|ja|大虫神社}} - A similarly named shrine in Echizen, Fukui Prefecture.
== References == * "Kaya Town History" Volumes 1 & 2, Kaya Town Office, 1974 & 2007. * "Shikinaisha Research Reports, Vol. 18," Kōgakkan University Press, 1984. * "Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places," Vol. 7, Heibonsha, 2000. * "Place names of Kyoto Prefecture," Heibonsha, 1981.
== External Links == * [https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/jinja/300401_53204/ Ōmushi Shrine - Kokugakuin Digital Museum]

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Ōmushi Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:与謝野町 大虫神社8.jpg|300px]] | location = Atsue, Yosano Town, Yosa District, Kyoto Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|29|36.3|N|135|07|03.3|E|scale:25000}} | deity = Ōnamuchi no Mikoto | shrine_rank = Shikinaisha (Myōjin Taisha), Former Fusha | founded = Unknown | architecture = Gable style (Kirimitsukuri) | festivals = Last Sunday of April | rituals = Kayadani Festival (Last Saturday and Sunday of April) }}
'''Ōmushi Shrine''' (大虫神社, ''Ōmushi Jinja'') is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in Atsue, {{ill|Yosano Town|ja|与謝野町}}, Kyoto Prefecture, Japan. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myōjin Taisha) and a former {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}}. The shrine stands on an elevated plain to the east of the Kayadani region, a central area in the ancient {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後地方}}, known for its historical importance and dense clusters of ancient Kofuns and archaeological sites.
== Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大己貴命}} - Primary deity * {{ill|Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja|少童命}} - Enshrined following the 1881 merger of Atsue Shrine * {{ill|Ōta no Mikoto|ja|大田命}} - Enshrined following the merger of Tokura Shrine in 1881
== History == === Founding Myths === The shrine was once located on the mid-slopes of {{ill|Mount Ōe|ja|大江山}} at a site called Ikeganaru, along with the nearby {{ill|Komushi Shrine|ja|小虫神社}}. It was popularly known as "Mushi no Miya" (Shrine of Insects). According to legend, when Ōnamuchi no Mikoto and {{ill|Numakawa-hime|ja|沼河姫}} resided in the area, a malevolent demon named Tsuchi Oni appear[[Category:11 to pp]]ed, spreading illness. With the intervention of {{ill|Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命}}, the demon was driven away, but the aftermath caused widespread affliction among humans and animals. To mitigate this, Sukunahikona no Mikoto became known as "Komushi" (Small Insect), purging illness from within, while Ōnamuchi no Mikoto was called "Ōmushi" (Large Insect), curing diseases externally. This narrative led to the worship of both as deities of healing.
=== Heian Period and Beyond === Historical records in the {{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|ja|日本文徳天皇実録}} note the shrine's elevation to the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, in 855 CE. The shrine is also listed in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} as a Myōjin Taisha and appear[[Category:11 to pp]]s as "Shōichi-i Ōmushi Myōjin" in the surviving {{ill|Tango Province Shrine Register|ja|丹後国神名帳}}. During the Kamakura period, it controlled over 18 hectares of shrine lands, remnants of which remain as local names such as "Gokuden" (Offering Fields) and "Tomyōden" (Lamp Fields).
In the early Muromachi period, the shrine was relocated to its current site, though the reasons remain unclear. The site lies in the historical Achi-e District, an advanced area in ancient Tango with connections to major transport routes. The shrine's status as a revered local protector persisted through the Edo period, with additional legends linking it to figures such as {{ill|Prince Maroko|ja|麻呂子親王}} and King Ōke and Kōke (future emperors).
=== Modern Period === The shrine was designated as a village shrine in 1873 and later promoted to Fusha in 1918. It underwent reconstruction after a fire in 1877 and subsequently absorbed two smaller local shrines, Atsue Shrine and Tokura Shrine. The shrine remains part of the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}.
== festivals == The shrine is central to the {{ill|Kayadani Festival|ja|加悦谷祭}}, a joint event with Komushi Shrine, involving ritual dances and processions. Highlights include: * '''Taiko Drum Performances:''' Traditional drumming to honor the deities. * '''Sword Dances:''' Skilled displays by children and youth participants. * '''Processions and Offerings:''' Sacred objects are paraded and presented at both shrines.
== Cultural Properties == * '''Five-story Stone Pagoda''' (1982, Yosano Town-designated cultural property) * '''Fragments of Buddhist Sutras:''' Excavated from the precincts' sutra mound, including references to "Taira-no-Yasuji," a local historical figure.
== See Also == * {{ill|Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)|ja|大虫神社}} - A similarly named shrine in Echizen, Fukui Prefecture.
== References == * "Kaya Town History" Volumes 1 & 2, Kaya Town Office, 1974 & 2007. * "Shikinaisha Research Reports, Vol. 18," Kōgakkan University Press, 1984. * "Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places," Vol. 7, Heibonsha, 2000. * "Place names of Kyoto Prefecture," Heibonsha, 1981.
== External Links == * [https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/jinja/300401_53204/ Ōmushi Shrine - Kokugakuin Digital Museum]

== History == === Founding Myths === The shrine was once located on the mid-slopes of {{ill|Mount Ōe|ja|大江山}} at a site called Ikeganaru, along with the nearby {{ill|Komushi Shrine|ja|小虫神社}}. It was popularly known as "Mushi no Miya" (Shrine of Insects). According to legend, when Ōnamuchi no Mikoto and {{ill|Numakawa-hime|ja|沼河姫}} resided in the area, a malevolent demon named Tsuchi Oni appear[[Category:pp to pp]]ed, spreading illness. With the intervention of {{ill|Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命}}, the demon was driven away, but the aftermath caused widespread affliction among humans and animals. To mitigate this, Sukunahikona no Mikoto became known as "Komushi" (Small Insect), purging illness from within, while Ōnamuchi no Mikoto was called "Ōmushi" (Large Insect), curing diseases externally. This narrative led to the worship of both as deities of healing.
=== Heian Period and Beyond === Historical records in the {{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|ja|日本文徳天皇実録}} note the shrine's elevation to the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, in 855 CE. The shrine is also listed in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} as a Myōjin Taisha and appear[[Category:pp to pp]]s as "Shōichi-i Ōmushi Myōjin" in the surviving {{ill|Tango Province Shrine Register|ja|丹後国神名帳}}. During the Kamakura period, it controlled over 18 hectares of shrine lands, remnants of which remain as local names such as "Gokuden" (Offering Fields) and "Tomyōden" (Lamp Fields).
In the early Muromachi period, the shrine was relocated to its current site, though the reasons remain unclear. The site lies in the historical Achi-e District, an advanced area in ancient Tango with connections to major transport routes. The shrine's status as a revered local protector persisted through the Edo period, with additional legends linking it to figures such as {{ill|Prince Maroko|ja|麻呂子親王}} and King Ōke and Kōke (future emperors).
=== Modern Period === The shrine was designated as a village shrine in 1873 and later promoted to Fusha in 1918. It underwent reconstruction after a fire in 1877 and subsequently absorbed two smaller local shrines, Atsue Shrine and Tokura Shrine. The shrine remains part of the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}.
== festivals == The shrine is central to the {{ill|Kayadani Festival|ja|加悦谷祭}}, a joint event with Komushi Shrine, involving ritual dances and processions. Highlights include: * '''Taiko Drum Performances:''' Traditional drumming to honor the deities. * '''Sword Dances:''' Skilled displays by children and youth participants. * '''Processions and Offerings:''' Sacred objects are paraded and presented at both shrines.
== Cultural Properties == * '''Five-story Stone Pagoda''' (1982, Yosano Town-designated cultural property) * '''Fragments of Buddhist Sutras:''' Excavated from the precincts' sutra mound, including references to "Taira-no-Yasuji," a local historical figure.
== See Also == * {{ill|Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)|ja|大虫神社}} - A similarly named shrine in Echizen, Fukui Prefecture.
== References == * "Kaya Town History" Volumes 1 & 2, Kaya Town Office, 1974 & 2007. * "Shikinaisha Research Reports, Vol. 18," Kōgakkan University Press, 1984. * "Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places," Vol. 7, Heibonsha, 2000. * "Place names of Kyoto Prefecture," Heibonsha, 1981.

{{Infobox religious building | name = Ōmushi Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:与謝野町 大虫神社8.jpg|300px]] | location = Atsue, Yosano Town, Yosa District, Kyoto Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|29|36.3|N|135|07|03.3|E|scale:25000}} | deity = Ōnamuchi no Mikoto | shrine_rank = Shikinaisha (Myōjin Taisha), Former Fusha | founded = Unknown | architecture = Gable style (Kirimitsukuri) | festivals = Last Sunday of April | rituals = Kayadani Festival (Last Saturday and Sunday of April) }}
'''Ōmushi Shrine''' (大虫神社, ''Ōmushi Jinja'') is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in Atsue, {{ill|Yosano Town|ja|与謝野町}}, Kyoto Prefecture, Japan. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myōjin Taisha) and a former {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}}. The shrine stands on an elevated plain to the east of the Kayadani region, a central area in the ancient {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後地方}}, known for its historical importance and dense clusters of ancient Kofuns and archaeological sites.
== Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大己貴命}} - Primary deity * {{ill|Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja|少童命}} - Enshrined following the 1881 merger of Atsue Shrine * {{ill|Ōta no Mikoto|ja|大田命}} - Enshrined following the merger of Tokura Shrine in 1881
== History == === Founding Myths === The shrine was once located on the mid-slopes of {{ill|Mount Ōe|ja|大江山}} at a site called Ikeganaru, along with the nearby {{ill|Komushi Shrine|ja|小虫神社}}. It was popularly known as "Mushi no Miya" (Shrine of Insects). According to legend, when Ōnamuchi no Mikoto and {{ill|Numakawa-hime|ja|沼河姫}} resided in the area, a malevolent demon named Tsuchi Oni appear[[Category:pp to pp]]ed, spreading illness. With the intervention of {{ill|Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命}}, the demon was driven away, but the aftermath caused widespread affliction among humans and animals. To mitigate this, Sukunahikona no Mikoto became known as "Komushi" (Small Insect), purging illness from within, while Ōnamuchi no Mikoto was called "Ōmushi" (Large Insect), curing diseases externally. This narrative led to the worship of both as deities of healing.
=== Heian Period and Beyond === Historical records in the {{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|ja|日本文徳天皇実録}} note the shrine's elevation to the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, in 855 CE. The shrine is also listed in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} as a Myōjin Taisha and appear[[Category:pp to pp]]s as "Shōichi-i Ōmushi Myōjin" in the surviving {{ill|Tango Province Shrine Register|ja|丹後国神名帳}}. During the Kamakura period, it controlled over 18 hectares of shrine lands, remnants of which remain as local names such as "Gokuden" (Offering Fields) and "Tomyōden" (Lamp Fields).
In the early Muromachi period, the shrine was relocated to its current site, though the reasons remain unclear. The shrine's status as a revered local protector persisted through the Edo period, with additional legends linking it to figures such as {{ill|Prince Maroko|ja|麻呂子親王}} and King Ōke and Kōke (future emperors).

It underwent reconstruction after a fire in 1877 and subsequently absorbed two smaller local shrines, Atsue Shrine and Tokura Shrine. The shrine remains part of the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}.
