'''Ōmushi Shrine''' (大虫神社, ''Ōmushi Jinja'') is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in Atsue, [[:en:Yosano, Kyoto|Yosano Town]], Kyoto Prefecture, Japan. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}} (Myōjin Taisha) and a former {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社|lt=Fu-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#Metropolitan_and_Prefectural_Shrines|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度}}. The shrine stands on an elevated plain to the east of the Kayadani region, a central area in the ancient {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後地方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tango Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 丹後国}}, known for its historical importance and dense clusters of ancient Kofuns and archaeological sites.
== Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大己貴命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} - Primary deity * {{ill|Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja|少童命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ワタツミ}} - Enshrined following the 1881 merger of Atsue Shrine * {{ill|Ōta no Mikoto|ja|大田命|1=Ōta-no-Mikoto|2=ja|3=太田命|4=fr|5=大田命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōta no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōta-no-Mikoto|qq=}} - Enshrined following the merger of Tokura Shrine in 1881
== History == === Founding Myths === The shrine was once located on the mid-slopes of [[:en:Ōeyama (mountain range)|Mount Ōe]] at a site called Ikeganaru, along with the nearby {{ill|Komushi Shrine|ja|小虫神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komushi Shrine|qq=}}. It was popularly known as "Mushi no Miya" (Shrine of Insects). According to legend, when Ōnamuchi no Mikoto and {{ill|Nunakawahime|lt=Nunakawahime|lt=Numakawa-hime|ja|沼河姫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Numakawa-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 沼河比売}} resided in the area, a malevolent demon named Tsuchi Oni appeared, spreading illness. With the intervention of {{ill|Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, the demon was driven away, but the aftermath caused widespread affliction among humans and animals. To mitigate this, Sukunahikona no Mikoto became known as "Komushi" (Small Insect), purging illness from within, while Ōnamuchi no Mikoto was called "Ōmushi" (Large Insect), curing diseases externally. This narrative led to the worship of both as deities of healing.
=== Heian Period and Beyond === Historical records in the [[:en:Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]] note the shrine's elevation to the Rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, in 855 CE. The shrine is also listed in the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]] as a Myōjin Taisha and appears as "Shōichi-i Ōmushi Myōjin" in the surviving {{ill|Tango Province Shrine Register|ja|丹後国神名帳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tango Province Shrine Register|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. During the Kamakura period, it controlled over 18 hectares of shrine lands, remnants of which remain as local names such as "Gokuden" (Offering Fields) and "Tomyōden" (Lamp Fields).
In the early Muromachi period, the shrine was relocated to its current site, though the reasons remain unclear. The site lies in the historical Achi-e District, an advanced area in ancient Tango with connections to major transport routes. The shrine's status as a revered local protector persisted through the Edo period, with additional legends linking it to figures such as {{ill|Prince Maroko|ja|麻呂子親王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Maroko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 当麻皇子}} and King Ōke and Kōke (future emperors).
=== Modern Period === The shrine was designated as a village shrine in 1873 and later promoted to Fusha in 1918. It underwent reconstruction after a fire in 1877 and subsequently absorbed two smaller local shrines, Atsue Shrine and Tokura Shrine. The shrine remains part of the [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]].
== festivals == The shrine is central to the {{ill|Kayadani Festival|ja|加悦谷祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kayadani Festival|qq=}}, a joint event with Komushi Shrine, involving ritual dances and processions. Highlights include: * '''Taiko Drum Performances:''' Traditional drumming to honor the deities. * '''Sword Dances:''' Skilled displays by children and youth participants. * '''Processions and Offerings:''' Sacred objects are paraded and presented at both shrines.
== Cultural Properties == * '''Five-story Stone Pagoda''' (1982, Yosano Town-designated cultural property) * '''Fragments of Buddhist Sutras:''' Excavated from the precincts' sutra mound, including references to "Taira-no-Yasuji," a local historical figure. == See Also == * {{ill|Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)|ja|大虫神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)|qq=}} - A similarly named shrine in Echizen, Fukui Prefecture.
== References == * "Kaya Town History" Volumes 1 & 2, Kaya Town Office, 1974 & 2007. * "Shikinaisha Research Reports, Vol. 18," Kōgakkan University Press, 1984. * "Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places," Vol. 7, Heibonsha, 2000. * "Place names of Kyoto Prefecture," Heibonsha, 1981.
== External Links == * [https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/jinja/300401_53204/ Ōmushi Shrine - Kokugakuin Digital Museum]

revision.comment: Merge [[Category:与謝野の Municipal History]] into [[Category:History of Yosano]] via bot
revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Ōmushi Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:与謝野町 大虫神社8.jpg|300px]] | location = Atsue, Yosano Town, Yosa District, Kyoto Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|29|36.3|N|135|07|03.3|E|scale:25000}} | deity = Ōnamuchi no Mikoto | shrine_Rank = Shikinaisha (Myōjin Taisha), Former Fu-sha | founded = Unknown | architecture = Gable style (Kirimitsukuri) | festivals = Last Sunday of April | rituals = Kayadani Festival (Last Saturday and Sunday of April) }}
'''Ōmushi Shrine''' (大虫神社, ''Ōmushi Jinja'') is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in Atsue, [[:en:Yosano, Kyoto|Yosano Town]], Kyoto Prefecture, Japan. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} (Myōjin Taisha) and a former {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社|lt=Fu-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#Metropolitan_and_Prefectural_Shrines|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度}}. The shrine stands on an elevated plain to the east of the Kayadani region, a central area in the ancient {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後地方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tango Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 丹後国}}, known for its historical importance and dense clusters of ancient Kofuns and archaeological sites.
== Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大己貴命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} - Primary deity * {{ill|Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja|少童命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ワタツミ}} - Enshrined following the 1881 merger of Atsue Shrine * {{ill|Ōta no Mikoto|ja|大田命|1=Ōta-no-Mikoto|2=ja|3=太田命|4=fr|5=大田命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōta no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōta-no-Mikoto|qq=}} - Enshrined following the merger of Tokura Shrine in 1881
== History == === Founding Myths === The shrine was once located on the mid-slopes of [[:en:Ōeyama (mountain range)|Mount Ōe]] at a site called Ikeganaru, along with the nearby {{ill|Komushi Shrine|ja|小虫神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komushi Shrine|qq=}}. It was popularly known as "Mushi no Miya" (Shrine of Insects). According to legend, when Ōnamuchi no Mikoto and {{ill|Nunakawahime|lt=Nunakawahime|lt=Numakawa-hime|ja|沼河姫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Numakawa-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 沼河比売}} resided in the area, a malevolent demon named Tsuchi Oni appeared, spreading illness. With the intervention of {{ill|Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, the demon was driven away, but the aftermath caused widespread affliction among humans and animals. To mitigate this, Sukunahikona no Mikoto became known as "Komushi" (Small Insect), purging illness from within, while Ōnamuchi no Mikoto was called "Ōmushi" (Large Insect), curing diseases externally. This narrative led to the worship of both as deities of healing.
=== Heian Period and Beyond === Historical records in the [[:en:Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]] note the shrine's elevation to the Rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, in 855 CE. The shrine is also listed in the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]] as a Myōjin Taisha and appears as "Shōichi-i Ōmushi Myōjin" in the surviving {{ill|Tango Province Shrine Register|ja|丹後国神名帳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tango Province Shrine Register|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. During the Kamakura period, it controlled over 18 hectares of shrine lands, remnants of which remain as local names such as "Gokuden" (Offering Fields) and "Tomyōden" (Lamp Fields).
In the early Muromachi period, the shrine was relocated to its current site, though the reasons remain unclear. The site lies in the historical Achi-e District, an advanced area in ancient Tango with connections to major transport routes. The shrine's status as a revered local protector persisted through the Edo period, with additional legends linking it to figures such as {{ill|Prince Maroko|ja|麻呂子親王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Maroko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 当麻皇子}} and King Ōke and Kōke (future emperors).
=== Modern Period === The shrine was designated as a village shrine in 1873 and later promoted to Fusha in 1918. It underwent reconstruction after a fire in 1877 and subsequently absorbed two smaller local shrines, Atsue Shrine and Tokura Shrine. The shrine remains part of the [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]].
== festivals == The shrine is central to the {{ill|Kayadani Festival|ja|加悦谷祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kayadani Festival|qq=}}, a joint event with Komushi Shrine, involving ritual dances and processions. Highlights include: * '''Taiko Drum Performances:''' Traditional drumming to honor the deities. * '''Sword Dances:''' Skilled displays by children and youth participants. * '''Processions and Offerings:''' Sacred objects are paraded and presented at both shrines.
== Cultural Properties == * '''Five-story Stone Pagoda''' (1982, Yosano Town-designated cultural property) * '''Fragments of Buddhist Sutras:''' Excavated from the precincts' sutra mound, including references to "Taira-no-Yasuji," a local historical figure. == See Also == * {{ill|Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)|ja|大虫神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)|qq=}} - A similarly named shrine in Echizen, Fukui Prefecture.
== References == * "Kaya Town History" Volumes 1 & 2, Kaya Town Office, 1974 & 2007. * "Shikinaisha Research Reports, Vol. 18," Kōgakkan University Press, 1984. * "Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places," Vol. 7, Heibonsha, 2000. * "Place names of Kyoto Prefecture," Heibonsha, 1981.
== External Links == * [https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/jinja/300401_53204/ Ōmushi Shrine - Kokugakuin Digital Museum]

== festivals == The shrine is central to the {{ill|Kayadani Festival|ja|加悦谷祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kayadani Festival|qq=}}, a joint event with Komushi Shrine, involving ritual dances and processions. Highlights include: * '''Taiko Drum Performances:''' Traditional drumming to honor the deities. * '''Sword Dances:''' Skilled displays by children and youth participants. * '''Processions and Offerings:''' Sacred objects are paraded and presented at both shrines.
== Cultural Properties == * '''Five-story Stone Pagoda''' (1982, Yosano Town-designated cultural property) * '''Fragments of Buddhist Sutras:''' Excavated from the precincts' sutra mound, including references to "Taira-no-Yasuji," a local historical figure. == See Also == * {{ill|Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)|ja|大虫神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)|qq=}} - A similarly named shrine in Echizen, Fukui Prefecture.
== References == * "Kaya Town History" Volumes 1 & 2, Kaya Town Office, 1974 & 2007. * "Shikinaisha Research Reports, Vol. 18," Kōgakkan University Press, 1984. * "Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places," Vol. 7, Heibonsha, 2000. * "Place names of Kyoto Prefecture," Heibonsha, 1981.
== External Links == * [https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/jinja/300401_53204/ Ōmushi Shrine - Kokugakuin Digital Museum]

revision.comment: Bot: sync infobox from Wikidata (P18,P131,P625,P112,P825,P361,P140,P149)
revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11438782]] Wikidata name: Omushi-jinja (Yosano) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Ōmushi Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:与謝野町 大虫神社8.jpg | location = Atsue, Yosano Town, Yosa District, Kyoto Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35.493417|N|135.117583|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = Ōnamuchi no Mikoto | shrine_Rank = Shikinaisha (Myōjin Taisha), Former Fu-sha | founded = Unknown | architecture = Gable style (Kirimitsukuri) | festivals = Last Sunday of April | rituals = Kayadani Festival (Last Saturday and Sunday of April) | prefecture= {{ill|Yosano|qid=Q1203259}} }}
'''Ōmushi Shrine''' (大虫神社, ''Ōmushi Jinja'') is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in Atsue, [[:en:Yosano, Kyoto|Yosano Town]], Kyoto Prefecture, Japan. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} (Myōjin Taisha) and a former {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社|lt=Fu-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#Metropolitan_and_Prefectural_Shrines|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度}}. The shrine stands on an elevated plain to the east of the Kayadani region, a central area in the ancient {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後地方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tango Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 丹後国}}, known for its historical importance and dense clusters of ancient Kofuns and archaeological sites.
== Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大己貴命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} - Primary deity * {{ill|Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja|少童命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ワタツミ}} - Enshrined following the 1881 merger of Atsue Shrine * {{ill|Ōta no Mikoto|ja|大田命|1=Ōta-no-Mikoto|2=ja|3=太田命|4=fr|5=大田命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōta no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōta-no-Mikoto|qq=}} - Enshrined following the merger of Tokura Shrine in 1881
== History == === Founding Myths === The shrine was once located on the mid-slopes of [[:en:Ōeyama (mountain range)|Mount Ōe]] at a site called Ikeganaru, along with the nearby {{ill|Komushi Shrine|ja|小虫神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komushi Shrine|qq=}}. It was popularly known as "Mushi no Miya" (Shrine of Insects). According to legend, when Ōnamuchi no Mikoto and {{ill|Nunakawahime|lt=Nunakawahime|lt=Numakawa-hime|ja|沼河姫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Numakawa-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 沼河比売}} resided in the area, a malevolent demon named Tsuchi Oni appeared, spreading illness. With the intervention of {{ill|Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, the demon was driven away, but the aftermath caused widespread affliction among humans and animals. To mitigate this, Sukunahikona no Mikoto became known as "Komushi" (Small Insect), purging illness from within, while Ōnamuchi no Mikoto was called "Ōmushi" (Large Insect), curing diseases externally. This narrative led to the worship of both as deities of healing.
=== Heian Period and Beyond === Historical records in the [[:en:Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]] note the shrine's elevation to the Rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, in 855 CE. The shrine is also listed in the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]] as a Myōjin Taisha and appears as "Shōichi-i Ōmushi Myōjin" in the surviving {{ill|Tango Province Shrine Register|ja|丹後国神名帳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tango Province Shrine Register|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. During the Kamakura period, it controlled over 18 hectares of shrine lands, remnants of which remain as local names such as "Gokuden" (Offering Fields) and "Tomyōden" (Lamp Fields).
In the early Muromachi period, the shrine was relocated to its current site, though the reasons remain unclear. The site lies in the historical Achi-e District, an advanced area in ancient Tango with connections to major transport routes. The shrine's status as a revered local protector persisted through the Edo period, with additional legends linking it to figures such as {{ill|Prince Maroko|ja|麻呂子親王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Maroko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 当麻皇子}} and King Ōke and Kōke (future emperors).
=== Modern Period === The shrine was designated as a village shrine in 1873 and later promoted to Fusha in 1918. It underwent reconstruction after a fire in 1877 and subsequently absorbed two smaller local shrines, Atsue Shrine and Tokura Shrine. The shrine remains part of the [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]].
== festivals == The shrine is central to the {{ill|Kayadani Festival|ja|加悦谷祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kayadani Festival|qq=}}, a joint event with Komushi Shrine, involving ritual dances and processions. Highlights include: * '''Taiko Drum Performances:''' Traditional drumming to honor the deities. * '''Sword Dances:''' Skilled displays by children and youth participants. * '''Processions and Offerings:''' Sacred objects are paraded and presented at both shrines.
== Cultural Properties == * '''Five-story Stone Pagoda''' (1982, Yosano Town-designated cultural property) * '''Fragments of Buddhist Sutras:''' Excavated from the precincts' sutra mound, including references to "Taira-no-Yasuji," a local historical figure. == See Also == * {{ill|Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)|ja|大虫神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)|qq=}} - A similarly named shrine in Echizen, Fukui Prefecture.
== References == * "Kaya Town History" Volumes 1 & 2, Kaya Town Office, 1974 & 2007. * "Shikinaisha Research Reports, Vol. 18," Kōgakkan University Press, 1984. * "Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places," Vol. 7, Heibonsha, 2000. * "Place names of Kyoto Prefecture," Heibonsha, 1981.
== External Links == * [https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/jinja/300401_53204/ Ōmushi Shrine - Kokugakuin Digital Museum]

It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} (Myōjin Taisha) and a former {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社|1=Metropolitan Shrine|qid=Q134917326}}{{efn|lt=Fu-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. The shrine stands on an elevated plain to the east of the Kayadani region, a central area in the ancient {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後地方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tango Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 丹後国|1=Tango Province|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1054331}}, known for its historical importance and dense clusters of ancient Kofuns and archaeological sites.
== Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大己貴命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} - Primary deity * {{ill|Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja|少童命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ワタツミ|1=Watatsumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1474187}} - Enshrined following the 1881 merger of Atsue Shrine * {{ill|Ōta no Mikoto|ja|大田命|1=Ōta-no-Mikoto|2=ja|3=太田命|4=fr|5=大田命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōta no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōta-no-Mikoto|qq=}} - Enshrined following the merger of Tokura Shrine in 1881
== History == === Founding Myths === The shrine was once located on the mid-slopes of [[:en:Ōeyama (mountain range)|Mount Ōe]] at a site called Ikeganaru, along with the nearby {{ill|Komushi Shrine|ja|小虫神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komushi Shrine|qq=}}. It was popularly known as "Mushi no Miya" (Shrine of Insects). According to legend, when Ōnamuchi no Mikoto and {{ill|Nunakawahime|lt=Nunakawahime|lt=Numakawa-hime|ja|沼河姫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Numakawa-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 沼河比売}} resided in the area, a malevolent demon named Tsuchi Oni appeared, spreading illness. With the intervention of {{ill|Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ|1=Sukunabikona|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3275687}}, the demon was driven away, but the aftermath caused widespread affliction among humans and animals. To mitigate this, Sukunahikona no Mikoto became known as "Komushi" (Small Insect), purging illness from within, while Ōnamuchi no Mikoto was called "Ōmushi" (Large Insect), curing diseases externally. This narrative led to the worship of both as deities of healing.
=== Heian Period and Beyond === Historical records in the [[:en:Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]] note the shrine's elevation to the Rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, in 855 CE. The shrine is also listed in the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]] as a Myōjin Taisha and appears as "Shōichi-i Ōmushi Myōjin" in the surviving {{ill|Tango Province Shrine Register|ja|丹後国神名帳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tango Province Shrine Register|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. During the Kamakura period, it controlled over 18 hectares of shrine lands, remnants of which remain as local names such as "Gokuden" (Offering Fields) and "Tomyōden" (Lamp Fields).
In the early Muromachi period, the shrine was relocated to its current site, though the reasons remain unclear. The site lies in the historical Achi-e District, an advanced area in ancient Tango with connections to major transport routes. The shrine's status as a revered local protector persisted through the Edo period, with additional legends linking it to figures such as {{ill|Prince Maroko|ja|麻呂子親王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Maroko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 当麻皇子}} and King Ōke and Kōke (future emperors).
=== Modern Period === The shrine was designated as a village shrine in 1873 and later promoted to Fusha in 1918. It underwent reconstruction after a fire in 1877 and subsequently absorbed two smaller local shrines, Atsue Shrine and Tokura Shrine. The shrine remains part of the [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]].
== festivals == The shrine is central to the {{ill|Kayadani Festival|ja|加悦谷祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kayadani Festival|qq=|1=A feast day of Kayadani|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q63795744}}, a joint event with Komushi Shrine, involving ritual dances and processions. Highlights include: * '''Taiko Drum Performances:''' Traditional drumming to honor the deities. * '''Sword Dances:''' Skilled displays by children and youth participants. * '''Processions and Offerings:''' Sacred objects are paraded and presented at both shrines.
== Cultural Properties == * '''Five-story Stone Pagoda''' (1982, Yosano Town-designated cultural property) * '''Fragments of Buddhist Sutras:''' Excavated from the precincts' sutra mound, including references to "Taira-no-Yasuji," a local historical figure. == See Also == * {{ill|Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)|ja|大虫神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)|qq=}} - A similarly named shrine in Echizen, Fukui Prefecture.
== References == * "Kaya Town History" Volumes 1 & 2, Kaya Town Office, 1974 & 2007. * "Shikinaisha Research Reports, Vol. 18," Kōgakkan University Press, 1984. * "Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places," Vol. 7, Heibonsha, 2000. * "Place names of Kyoto Prefecture," Heibonsha, 1981.
== External Links == * [https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/jinja/300401_53204/ Ōmushi Shrine - Kokugakuin Digital Museum]

== References == * "Kaya Town History" Volumes 1 & 2, Kaya Town Office, 1974 & 2007. * "Shikinaisha Research Reports, Vol. 18," Kōgakkan University Press, 1984. * "Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places," Vol. 7, Heibonsha, 2000. * "Place names of Kyoto Prefecture," Heibonsha, 1981.
== External Links == * [https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/jinja/300401_53204/ Ōmushi Shrine - Kokugakuin Digital Museum]

revision.comment: I realize that doing a recent edit on these ones is better than all of that other crap of manual cat a lot stuff
revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11438782]] Wikidata name: Omushi-jinja (Yosano) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Ōmushi Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:与謝野町 大虫神社8.jpg | location = Atsue, Yosano Town, Yosa District, Kyoto Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35.493417|N|135.117583|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = Ōnamuchi no Mikoto | shrine_Rank = Shikinaisha (Myōjin Taisha), Former Fu-sha | founded = Unknown | architecture = Gable style (Kirimitsukuri) | festivals = Last Sunday of April | rituals = Kayadani Festival (Last Saturday and Sunday of April) | prefecture= {{ill|Yosano|qid=Q1203259}} }}
'''Ōmushi Shrine''' (大虫神社, ''Ōmushi Jinja'') is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in Atsue, [[:en:Yosano, Kyoto|Yosano Town]], Kyoto Prefecture, Japan. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} (Myōjin Taisha) and a former {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社|1=Metropolitan Shrine|qid=Q134917326}}{{efn|lt=Fu-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. The shrine stands on an elevated plain to the east of the Kayadani region, a central area in the ancient {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後地方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tango Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 丹後国|1=Tango Province|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1054331}}, known for its historical importance and dense clusters of ancient Kofuns and archaeological sites.
== Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大己貴命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} - Primary deity * {{ill|Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja|少童命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ワタツミ|1=Watatsumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1474187}} - Enshrined following the 1881 merger of Atsue Shrine * {{ill|Ōta no Mikoto|ja|大田命|1=Ōta-no-Mikoto|2=ja|3=太田命|4=fr|5=大田命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōta no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōta-no-Mikoto|qq=}} - Enshrined following the merger of Tokura Shrine in 1881
== History == === Founding Myths === The shrine was once located on the mid-slopes of [[:en:Ōeyama (mountain range)|Mount Ōe]] at a site called Ikeganaru, along with the nearby {{ill|Komushi Shrine|ja|小虫神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komushi Shrine|qq=}}. It was popularly known as "Mushi no Miya" (Shrine of Insects). According to legend, when Ōnamuchi no Mikoto and {{ill|Nunakawahime|lt=Nunakawahime|lt=Numakawa-hime|ja|沼河姫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Numakawa-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 沼河比売}} resided in the area, a malevolent demon named Tsuchi Oni appeared, spreading illness. With the intervention of {{ill|Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ|1=Sukunabikona|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3275687}}, the demon was driven away, but the aftermath caused widespread affliction among humans and animals. To mitigate this, Sukunahikona no Mikoto became known as "Komushi" (Small Insect), purging illness from within, while Ōnamuchi no Mikoto was called "Ōmushi" (Large Insect), curing diseases externally. This narrative led to the worship of both as deities of healing.
=== Heian Period and Beyond === Historical records in the [[:en:Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]] note the shrine's elevation to the Rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, in 855 CE. The shrine is also listed in the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]] as a Myōjin Taisha and appears as "Shōichi-i Ōmushi Myōjin" in the surviving {{ill|Tango Province Shrine Register|ja|丹後国神名帳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tango Province Shrine Register|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. During the Kamakura period, it controlled over 18 hectares of shrine lands, remnants of which remain as local names such as "Gokuden" (Offering Fields) and "Tomyōden" (Lamp Fields).
In the early Muromachi period, the shrine was relocated to its current site, though the reasons remain unclear. The site lies in the historical Achi-e District, an advanced area in ancient Tango with connections to major transport routes. The shrine's status as a revered local protector persisted through the Edo period, with additional legends linking it to figures such as {{ill|Prince Maroko|ja|麻呂子親王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Maroko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 当麻皇子}} and King Ōke and Kōke (future emperors).
=== Modern Period === The shrine was designated as a village shrine in 1873 and later promoted to Fusha in 1918. It underwent reconstruction after a fire in 1877 and subsequently absorbed two smaller local shrines, Atsue Shrine and Tokura Shrine. The shrine remains part of the [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]].
== festivals == The shrine is central to the {{ill|Kayadani Festival|ja|加悦谷祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kayadani Festival|qq=|1=A feast day of Kayadani|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q63795744}}, a joint event with Komushi Shrine, involving ritual dances and processions. Highlights include: * '''Taiko Drum Performances:''' Traditional drumming to honor the deities. * '''Sword Dances:''' Skilled displays by children and youth participants. * '''Processions and Offerings:''' Sacred objects are paraded and presented at both shrines.
== Cultural Properties == * '''Five-story Stone Pagoda''' (1982, Yosano Town-designated cultural property) * '''Fragments of Buddhist Sutras:''' Excavated from the precincts' sutra mound, including references to "Taira-no-Yasuji," a local historical figure. == See Also == * {{ill|Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)|ja|大虫神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)|qq=}} - A similarly named shrine in Echizen, Fukui Prefecture.
== References == * "Kaya Town History" Volumes 1 & 2, Kaya Town Office, 1974 & 2007. * "Shikinaisha Research Reports, Vol. 18," Kōgakkan University Press, 1984. * "Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places," Vol. 7, Heibonsha, 2000. * "Place names of Kyoto Prefecture," Heibonsha, 1981.
== External Links == * [https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/jinja/300401_53204/ Ōmushi Shrine - Kokugakuin Digital Museum]
{{citation needed|ja|大虫神社|version=39256620|comment=Imported full ja history}}

It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} (Myōjin Taisha) and a former {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社|1=Metropolitan Shrine|qid=Q134917326}}{{efn|lt=Fu-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. The shrine stands on an elevated plain to the east of the Kayadani region, a central area in the ancient {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後地方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tango Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 丹後国|1=Tango Province|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1054331}}, known for its historical importance and dense clusters of ancient Kofuns and archaeological sites.
== Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大己貴命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} - Primary deity * {{ill|Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja|少童命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ワタツミ|1=Watatsumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1474187}} - Enshrined following the 1881 merger of Atsue Shrine * {{ill|Ōta no Mikoto|ja|大田命|1=Ōta-no-Mikoto|2=ja|3=太田命|4=fr|5=大田命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōta no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōta-no-Mikoto|qq=}} - Enshrined following the merger of Tokura Shrine in 1881
== History == === Founding Myths === The shrine was once located on the mid-slopes of [[:en:Ōeyama (mountain range)|Mount Ōe]] at a site called Ikeganaru, along with the nearby {{ill|Komushi Shrine|ja|小虫神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komushi Shrine|qq=}}. It was popularly known as "Mushi no Miya" (Shrine of Insects). According to legend, when Ōnamuchi no Mikoto and {{ill|Nunakawahime|lt=Nunakawahime|lt=Numakawa-hime|ja|沼河姫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Numakawa-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 沼河比売}} resided in the area, a malevolent demon named Tsuchi Oni appeared, spreading illness. With the intervention of {{ill|Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ|1=Sukunabikona|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3275687}}, the demon was driven away, but the aftermath caused widespread affliction among humans and animals. To mitigate this, Sukunahikona no Mikoto became known as "Komushi" (Small Insect), purging illness from within, while Ōnamuchi no Mikoto was called "Ōmushi" (Large Insect), curing diseases externally. This narrative led to the worship of both as deities of healing.
=== Heian Period and Beyond === Historical records in the [[:en:Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]] note the shrine's elevation to the Rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, in 855 CE. The shrine is also listed in the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]] as a Myōjin Taisha and appears as "Shōichi-i Ōmushi Myōjin" in the surviving {{ill|Tango Province Shrine Register|ja|丹後国神名帳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tango Province Shrine Register|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. During the Kamakura period, it controlled over 18 hectares of shrine lands, remnants of which remain as local names such as "Gokuden" (Offering Fields) and "Tomyōden" (Lamp Fields).
In the early Muromachi period, the shrine was relocated to its current site, though the reasons remain unclear. The site lies in the historical Achi-e District, an advanced area in ancient Tango with connections to major transport routes. The shrine's status as a revered local protector persisted through the Edo period, with additional legends linking it to figures such as {{ill|Prince Maroko|ja|麻呂子親王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Maroko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 当麻皇子}} and King Ōke and Kōke (future emperors).
=== Modern Period === The shrine was designated as a village shrine in 1873 and later promoted to Fusha in 1918. It underwent reconstruction after a fire in 1877 and subsequently absorbed two smaller local shrines, Atsue Shrine and Tokura Shrine. The shrine remains part of the [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]].
== festivals == The shrine is central to the {{ill|Kayadani Festival|ja|加悦谷祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kayadani Festival|qq=|1=A feast day of Kayadani|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q63795744}}, a joint event with Komushi Shrine, involving ritual dances and processions. Highlights include: * '''Taiko Drum Performances:''' Traditional drumming to honor the deities. * '''Sword Dances:''' Skilled displays by children and youth participants. * '''Processions and Offerings:''' Sacred objects are paraded and presented at both shrines.
== Cultural Properties == * '''Five-story Stone Pagoda''' (1982, Yosano Town-designated cultural property) * '''Fragments of Buddhist Sutras:''' Excavated from the precincts' sutra mound, including references to "Taira-no-Yasuji," a local historical figure. == See Also == * {{ill|Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)|ja|大虫神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)|qq=}} - A similarly named shrine in Echizen, Fukui Prefecture.
== References == * "Kaya Town History" Volumes 1 & 2, Kaya Town Office, 1974 & 2007. * "Shikinaisha Research Reports, Vol. 18," Kōgakkan University Press, 1984. * "Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places," Vol. 7, Heibonsha, 2000. * "Place names of Kyoto Prefecture," Heibonsha, 1981.
== External Links == * [https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/jinja/300401_53204/ Ōmushi Shrine - Kokugakuin Digital Museum]

The shrine stands on an elevated plain to the east of the Kayadani region, a central area in the ancient {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後地方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tango Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 丹後国|1=Tango Province|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1054331}}, known for its historical importance and dense clusters of ancient Kofuns and archaeological sites.

== History == === Founding Myths === The shrine was once located on the mid-slopes of [[:en:Ōeyama (mountain range)|Mount Ōe]] at a site called Ikeganaru, along with the nearby {{ill|Komushi Shrine|ja|小虫神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komushi Shrine|qq=}}. With the intervention of {{ill|Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ|1=Sukunabikona|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3275687}}, the demon was driven away, but the aftermath caused widespread affliction among humans and animals. This narrative led to the worship of both as deities of healing.

Highlights include: * '''Taiko Drum Performances:''' Traditional drumming to honor the deities. * '''Sword Dances:''' Skilled displays by children and youth participants. * '''Processions and Offerings:''' Sacred objects are paraded and presented at both shrines.

== References == * "Kaya Town History" Volumes 1 & 2, Kaya Town Office, 1974 & 2007. * "Shikinaisha Research Reports, Vol. 18," Kōgakkan University Press, 1984. * "Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places," Vol. 7, Heibonsha, 2000. * "Place names of Kyoto Prefecture," Heibonsha, 1981.
== External Links == * [https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/jinja/300401_53204/ Ōmushi Shrine - Kokugakuin Digital Museum]

revision.comment: Bot: remove incorrect Shona (sn:) interwiki links
revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11438782]] Wikidata name: Omushi-jinja (Yosano) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Ōmushi Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:与謝野町 大虫神社8.jpg | location = Atsue, Yosano Town, Yosa District, Kyoto Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35.493417|N|135.117583|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = Ōnamuchi no Mikoto | shrine_Rank = Shikinaisha (Myōjin Taisha), Former Fu-sha | founded = Unknown | architecture = Gable style (Kirimitsukuri) | festivals = Last Sunday of April | rituals = Kayadani Festival (Last Saturday and Sunday of April) | prefecture= {{ill|Yosano|qid=Q1203259}} }}
'''Ōmushi Shrine''' (大虫神社, ''Ōmushi Jinja'') is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in Atsue, [[:en:Yosano, Kyoto|Yosano Town]], Kyoto Prefecture, Japan. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} (Myōjin Taisha) and a former {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社|1=Metropolitan Shrine|qid=Q134917326}}{{efn|lt=Fu-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. The shrine stands on an elevated plain to the east of the Kayadani region, a central area in the ancient {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後地方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tango Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 丹後国|1=Tango Province|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1054331}}, known for its historical importance and dense clusters of ancient Kofuns and archaeological sites.
== Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大己貴命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} - Primary deity * {{ill|Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja|少童命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ワタツミ|1=Watatsumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1474187}} - Enshrined following the 1881 merger of Atsue Shrine * {{ill|Ōta no Mikoto|ja|大田命|1=Ōta-no-Mikoto|2=ja|3=太田命|4=fr|5=大田命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōta no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōta-no-Mikoto|qq=}} - Enshrined following the merger of Tokura Shrine in 1881
== History == === Founding Myths === The shrine was once located on the mid-slopes of [[:en:Ōeyama (mountain range)|Mount Ōe]] at a site called Ikeganaru, along with the nearby {{ill|Komushi Shrine|ja|小虫神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komushi Shrine|qq=}}. It was popularly known as "Mushi no Miya" (Shrine of Insects). According to legend, when Ōnamuchi no Mikoto and {{ill|Nunakawahime|lt=Nunakawahime|lt=Numakawa-hime|ja|沼河姫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Numakawa-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 沼河比売}} resided in the area, a malevolent demon named Tsuchi Oni appeared, spreading illness. With the intervention of {{ill|Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ|1=Sukunabikona|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3275687}}, the demon was driven away, but the aftermath caused widespread affliction among humans and animals. To mitigate this, Sukunahikona no Mikoto became known as "Komushi" (Small Insect), purging illness from within, while Ōnamuchi no Mikoto was called "Ōmushi" (Large Insect), curing diseases externally. This narrative led to the worship of both as deities of healing.
=== Heian Period and Beyond === Historical records in the [[:en:Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]] note the shrine's elevation to the Rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, in 855 CE. The shrine is also listed in the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]] as a Myōjin Taisha and appears as "Shōichi-i Ōmushi Myōjin" in the surviving {{ill|Tango Province Shrine Register|ja|丹後国神名帳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tango Province Shrine Register|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. During the Kamakura period, it controlled over 18 hectares of shrine lands, remnants of which remain as local names such as "Gokuden" (Offering Fields) and "Tomyōden" (Lamp Fields).
In the early Muromachi period, the shrine was relocated to its current site, though the reasons remain unclear. The site lies in the historical Achi-e District, an advanced area in ancient Tango with connections to major transport routes. The shrine's status as a revered local protector persisted through the Edo period, with additional legends linking it to figures such as {{ill|Prince Maroko|ja|麻呂子親王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Maroko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 当麻皇子}} and King Ōke and Kōke (future emperors).
=== Modern Period === The shrine was designated as a village shrine in 1873 and later promoted to Fusha in 1918. It underwent reconstruction after a fire in 1877 and subsequently absorbed two smaller local shrines, Atsue Shrine and Tokura Shrine. The shrine remains part of the [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]].
== festivals == The shrine is central to the {{ill|Kayadani Festival|ja|加悦谷祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kayadani Festival|qq=|1=A feast day of Kayadani|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q63795744}}, a joint event with Komushi Shrine, involving ritual dances and processions. Highlights include: * '''Taiko Drum Performances:''' Traditional drumming to honor the deities. * '''Sword Dances:''' Skilled displays by children and youth participants. * '''Processions and Offerings:''' Sacred objects are paraded and presented at both shrines.
== Cultural Properties == * '''Five-story Stone Pagoda''' (1982, Yosano Town-designated cultural property) * '''Fragments of Buddhist Sutras:''' Excavated from the precincts' sutra mound, including references to "Taira-no-Yasuji," a local historical figure. == See Also == * {{ill|Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)|ja|大虫神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)|qq=}} - A similarly named shrine in Echizen, Fukui Prefecture.
== References == * "Kaya Town History" Volumes 1 & 2, Kaya Town Office, 1974 & 2007. * "Shikinaisha Research Reports, Vol. 18," Kōgakkan University Press, 1984. * "Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places," Vol. 7, Heibonsha, 2000. * "Place names of Kyoto Prefecture," Heibonsha, 1981.
== External Links == * [https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/jinja/300401_53204/ Ōmushi Shrine - Kokugakuin Digital Museum]
{{citation needed|ja|大虫神社|version=39256620|comment=Imported full ja history}}

The shrine stands on an elevated plain to the east of the Kayadani region, a central area in the ancient {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後地方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tango Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 丹後国|1=Tango Province|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1054331}}, known for its historical importance and dense clusters of ancient Kofuns and archaeological sites.

== History == === Founding Myths === The shrine was once located on the mid-slopes of [[:en:Ōeyama (mountain range)|Mount Ōe]] at a site called Ikeganaru, along with the nearby {{ill|Komushi Shrine|ja|小虫神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komushi Shrine|qq=}}. It was popularly known as "Mushi no Miya" (Shrine of Insects). According to legend, when Ōnamuchi no Mikoto and {{ill|Nunakawahime|lt=Nunakawahime|lt=Numakawa-hime|ja|沼河姫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Numakawa-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 沼河比売}} resided in the area, a malevolent demon named Tsuchi Oni appeared, spreading illness. With the intervention of {{ill|Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ|1=Sukunabikona|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3275687}}, the demon was driven away, but the aftermath caused widespread affliction among humans and animals. To mitigate this, Sukunahikona no Mikoto became known as "Komushi" (Small Insect), purging illness from within, while Ōnamuchi no Mikoto was called "Ōmushi" (Large Insect), curing diseases externally. This narrative led to the worship of both as deities of healing.
=== Heian Period and Beyond === Historical records in the [[:en:Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]] note the shrine's elevation to the Rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, in 855 CE. The shrine is also listed in the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]] as a Myōjin Taisha and appears as "Shōichi-i Ōmushi Myōjin" in the surviving {{ill|Tango Province Shrine Register|ja|丹後国神名帳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tango Province Shrine Register|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. During the Kamakura period, it controlled over 18 hectares of shrine lands, remnants of which remain as local names such as "Gokuden" (Offering Fields) and "Tomyōden" (Lamp Fields).
In the early Muromachi period, the shrine was relocated to its current site, though the reasons remain unclear. The site lies in the historical Achi-e District, an advanced area in ancient Tango with connections to major transport routes. The shrine's status as a revered local protector persisted through the Edo period, with additional legends linking it to figures such as {{ill|Prince Maroko|ja|麻呂子親王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Maroko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 当麻皇子}} and King Ōke and Kōke (future emperors).
=== Modern Period === The shrine was designated as a village shrine in 1873 and later promoted to Fusha in 1918. It underwent reconstruction after a fire in 1877 and subsequently absorbed two smaller local shrines, Atsue Shrine and Tokura Shrine. The shrine remains part of the [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]].
== festivals == The shrine is central to the {{ill|Kayadani Festival|ja|加悦谷祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kayadani Festival|qq=|1=A feast day of Kayadani|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q63795744}}, a joint event with Komushi Shrine, involving ritual dances and processions. Highlights include: * '''Taiko Drum Performances:''' Traditional drumming to honor the deities. * '''Sword Dances:''' Skilled displays by children and youth participants. * '''Processions and Offerings:''' Sacred objects are paraded and presented at both shrines.
== Cultural Properties == * '''Five-story Stone Pagoda''' (1982, Yosano Town-designated cultural property) * '''Fragments of Buddhist Sutras:''' Excavated from the precincts' sutra mound, including references to "Taira-no-Yasuji," a local historical figure. == See Also == * {{ill|Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)|ja|大虫神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)|qq=}} - A similarly named shrine in Echizen, Fukui Prefecture.
== References == * "Kaya Town History" Volumes 1 & 2, Kaya Town Office, 1974 & 2007. * "Shikinaisha Research Reports, Vol. 18," Kōgakkan University Press, 1984. * "Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places," Vol. 7, Heibonsha, 2000. * "Place names of Kyoto Prefecture," Heibonsha, 1981.
== External Links == * [https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/jinja/300401_53204/ Ōmushi Shrine - Kokugakuin Digital Museum]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Omushi-jinja (Yosano)]][[ja:大虫神社 (与謝野町)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11438782]] Wikidata name: Omushi-jinja (Yosano) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Ōmushi Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:与謝野町 大虫神社8.jpg | location = Atsue, Yosano Town, Yosa District, Kyoto Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35.493417|N|135.117583|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = Ōnamuchi no Mikoto | shrine_Rank = Shikinaisha (Myōjin Taisha), Former Fu-sha | founded = Unknown | architecture = Gable style (Kirimitsukuri) | festivals = Last Sunday of April | rituals = Kayadani Festival (Last Saturday and Sunday of April) | prefecture= {{ill|Yosano|qid=Q1203259}} }}
'''Ōmushi Shrine''' (大虫神社, ''Ōmushi Jinja'') is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in Atsue, [[:en:Yosano, Kyoto|Yosano Town]], Kyoto Prefecture, Japan. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} (Myōjin Taisha) and a former {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社|1=Metropolitan Shrine|qid=Q134917326}}{{efn|lt=Fu-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. The shrine stands on an elevated plain to the east of the Kayadani region, a central area in the ancient {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後地方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tango Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 丹後国|1=Tango Province|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1054331}}, known for its historical importance and dense clusters of ancient Kofuns and archaeological sites.
== Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大己貴命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} - Primary deity * {{ill|Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja|少童命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ワタツミ|1=Watatsumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1474187}} - Enshrined following the 1881 merger of Atsue Shrine * {{ill|Ōta no Mikoto|ja|大田命|1=Ōta-no-Mikoto|2=ja|3=太田命|4=fr|5=大田命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōta no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōta-no-Mikoto|qq=}} - Enshrined following the merger of Tokura Shrine in 1881
== History == === Founding Myths === The shrine was once located on the mid-slopes of [[:en:Ōeyama (mountain range)|Mount Ōe]] at a site called Ikeganaru, along with the nearby {{ill|Komushi Shrine|ja|小虫神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komushi Shrine|qq=}}. It was popularly known as "Mushi no Miya" (Shrine of Insects). According to legend, when Ōnamuchi no Mikoto and {{ill|Nunakawahime|lt=Nunakawahime|lt=Numakawa-hime|ja|沼河姫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Numakawa-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 沼河比売}} resided in the area, a malevolent demon named Tsuchi Oni appeared, spreading illness. With the intervention of {{ill|Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ|1=Sukunabikona|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3275687}}, the demon was driven away, but the aftermath caused widespread affliction among humans and animals. To mitigate this, Sukunahikona no Mikoto became known as "Komushi" (Small Insect), purging illness from within, while Ōnamuchi no Mikoto was called "Ōmushi" (Large Insect), curing diseases externally. This narrative led to the worship of both as deities of healing.
=== Heian Period and Beyond === Historical records in the [[:en:Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]] note the shrine's elevation to the Rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, in 855 CE. The shrine is also listed in the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]] as a Myōjin Taisha and appears as "Shōichi-i Ōmushi Myōjin" in the surviving {{ill|Tango Province Shrine Register|ja|丹後国神名帳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tango Province Shrine Register|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. During the Kamakura period, it controlled over 18 hectares of shrine lands, remnants of which remain as local names such as "Gokuden" (Offering Fields) and "Tomyōden" (Lamp Fields).
In the early Muromachi period, the shrine was relocated to its current site, though the reasons remain unclear. The site lies in the historical Achi-e District, an advanced area in ancient Tango with connections to major transport routes. The shrine's status as a revered local protector persisted through the Edo period, with additional legends linking it to figures such as {{ill|Prince Maroko|ja|麻呂子親王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Maroko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 当麻皇子}} and King Ōke and Kōke (future emperors).
=== Modern Period === The shrine was designated as a village shrine in 1873 and later promoted to Fusha in 1918. It underwent reconstruction after a fire in 1877 and subsequently absorbed two smaller local shrines, Atsue Shrine and Tokura Shrine. The shrine remains part of the [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]].
== festivals == The shrine is central to the {{ill|Kayadani Festival|ja|加悦谷祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kayadani Festival|qq=|1=A feast day of Kayadani|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q63795744}}, a joint event with Komushi Shrine, involving ritual dances and processions. Highlights include: * '''Taiko Drum Performances:''' Traditional drumming to honor the deities. * '''Sword Dances:''' Skilled displays by children and youth participants. * '''Processions and Offerings:''' Sacred objects are paraded and presented at both shrines.
== Cultural Properties == * '''Five-story Stone Pagoda''' (1982, Yosano Town-designated cultural property) * '''Fragments of Buddhist Sutras:''' Excavated from the precincts' sutra mound, including references to "Taira-no-Yasuji," a local historical figure. == See Also == * {{ill|Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)|ja|大虫神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)|qq=}} - A similarly named shrine in Echizen, Fukui Prefecture.
== References == * "Kaya Town History" Volumes 1 & 2, Kaya Town Office, 1974 & 2007. * "Shikinaisha Research Reports, Vol. 18," Kōgakkan University Press, 1984. * "Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places," Vol. 7, Heibonsha, 2000. * "Place names of Kyoto Prefecture," Heibonsha, 1981.
== External Links == * [https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/jinja/300401_53204/ Ōmushi Shrine - Kokugakuin Digital Museum]
{{citation needed|ja|大虫神社|version=39256620|comment=Imported full ja history}}

According to legend, when Ōnamuchi no Mikoto and {{ill|Nunakawahime|lt=Nunakawahime|lt=Numakawa-hime|ja|沼河姫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Numakawa-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 沼河比売}} resided in the area, a malevolent demon named Tsuchi Oni appeared, spreading illness. With the intervention of {{ill|Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ|1=Sukunabikona|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3275687}}, the demon was driven away, but the aftermath caused widespread affliction among humans and animals. To mitigate this, Sukunahikona no Mikoto became known as "Komushi" (Small Insect), purging illness from within, while Ōnamuchi no Mikoto was called "Ōmushi" (Large Insect), curing diseases externally. This narrative led to the worship of both as deities of healing.
=== Heian Period and Beyond === Historical records in the [[:en:Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]] note the shrine's elevation to the Rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, in 855 CE. The shrine is also listed in the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]] as a Myōjin Taisha and appears as "Shōichi-i Ōmushi Myōjin" in the surviving {{ill|Tango Province Shrine Register|ja|丹後国神名帳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tango Province Shrine Register|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. During the Kamakura period, it controlled over 18 hectares of shrine lands, remnants of which remain as local names such as "Gokuden" (Offering Fields) and "Tomyōden" (Lamp Fields).
In the early Muromachi period, the shrine was relocated to its current site, though the reasons remain unclear. The site lies in the historical Achi-e District, an advanced area in ancient Tango with connections to major transport routes. The shrine's status as a revered local protector persisted through the Edo period, with additional legends linking it to figures such as {{ill|Prince Maroko|ja|麻呂子親王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Maroko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 当麻皇子}} and King Ōke and Kōke (future emperors).
=== Modern Period === The shrine was designated as a village shrine in 1873 and later promoted to Fusha in 1918. It underwent reconstruction after a fire in 1877 and subsequently absorbed two smaller local shrines, Atsue Shrine and Tokura Shrine. The shrine remains part of the [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]].
== festivals == The shrine is central to the {{ill|Kayadani Festival|ja|加悦谷祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kayadani Festival|qq=|1=A feast day of Kayadani|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q63795744}}, a joint event with Komushi Shrine, involving ritual dances and processions. Highlights include: * '''Taiko Drum Performances:''' Traditional drumming to honor the deities. * '''Sword Dances:''' Skilled displays by children and youth participants. * '''Processions and Offerings:''' Sacred objects are paraded and presented at both shrines.
== Cultural Properties == * '''Five-story Stone Pagoda''' (1982, Yosano Town-designated cultural property) * '''Fragments of Buddhist Sutras:''' Excavated from the precincts' sutra mound, including references to "Taira-no-Yasuji," a local historical figure. == See Also == * {{ill|Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)|ja|大虫神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)|qq=}} - A similarly named shrine in Echizen, Fukui Prefecture.
== References == * "Kaya Town History" Volumes 1 & 2, Kaya Town Office, 1974 & 2007. * "Shikinaisha Research Reports, Vol. 18," Kōgakkan University Press, 1984. * "Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places," Vol. 7, Heibonsha, 2000. * "Place names of Kyoto Prefecture," Heibonsha, 1981.
== External Links == * [https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/jinja/300401_53204/ Ōmushi Shrine - Kokugakuin Digital Museum]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Omushi-jinja (Yosano)]][[ja:大虫神社 (与謝野町)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11438782]] Wikidata name: Omushi-jinja (Yosano) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Ōmushi Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:与謝野町 大虫神社8.jpg | location = Atsue, Yosano Town, Yosa District, Kyoto Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35.493417|N|135.117583|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = Ōnamuchi no Mikoto | shrine_Rank = Shikinaisha (Myōjin Taisha), Former Fu-sha | founded = Unknown | architecture = Gable style (Kirimitsukuri) | festivals = Last Sunday of April | rituals = Kayadani Festival (Last Saturday and Sunday of April) | prefecture= {{ill|Yosano|WD=Q1203259}} }}
'''Ōmushi Shrine''' (大虫神社, ''Ōmushi Jinja'') is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in Atsue, [[:en:Yosano, Kyoto|Yosano Town]], Kyoto Prefecture, Japan. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} (Myōjin Taisha) and a former {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社|1=Metropolitan Shrine|WD=Q134917326}}{{efn|lt=Fu-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. The shrine stands on an elevated plain to the east of the Kayadani region, a central area in the ancient {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後地方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tango Province|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 丹後国|1=Tango Province|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1054331}}, known for its historical importance and dense clusters of ancient Kofuns and archaeological sites.
== Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大己貴命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} - Primary deity * {{ill|Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja|少童命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ワタツミ|1=Watatsumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1474187}} - Enshrined following the 1881 merger of Atsue Shrine * {{ill|Ōta no Mikoto|ja|大田命|1=Ōta-no-Mikoto|2=ja|3=太田命|4=fr|5=大田命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōta no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōta-no-Mikoto|qq=}} - Enshrined following the merger of Tokura Shrine in 1881
== History == === Founding Myths === The shrine was once located on the mid-slopes of [[:en:Ōeyama (mountain range)|Mount Ōe]] at a site called Ikeganaru, along with the nearby {{ill|Komushi Shrine|ja|小虫神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komushi Shrine|qq=}}. It was popularly known as "Mushi no Miya" (Shrine of Insects). According to legend, when Ōnamuchi no Mikoto and {{ill|Nunakawahime|lt=Nunakawahime|lt=Numakawa-hime|ja|沼河姫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Numakawa-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 沼河比売}} resided in the area, a malevolent demon named Tsuchi Oni appeared, spreading illness. With the intervention of {{ill|Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ|1=Sukunabikona|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3275687}}, the demon was driven away, but the aftermath caused widespread affliction among humans and animals. To mitigate this, Sukunahikona no Mikoto became known as "Komushi" (Small Insect), purging illness from within, while Ōnamuchi no Mikoto was called "Ōmushi" (Large Insect), curing diseases externally. This narrative led to the worship of both as deities of healing.
=== Heian Period and Beyond === Historical records in the [[:en:Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]] note the shrine's elevation to the Rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, in 855 CE. The shrine is also listed in the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]] as a Myōjin Taisha and appears as "Shōichi-i Ōmushi Myōjin" in the surviving {{ill|Tango Province Shrine Register|ja|丹後国神名帳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tango Province Shrine Register|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. During the Kamakura period, it controlled over 18 hectares of shrine lands, remnants of which remain as local names such as "Gokuden" (Offering Fields) and "Tomyōden" (Lamp Fields).
In the early Muromachi period, the shrine was relocated to its current site, though the reasons remain unclear. The site lies in the historical Achi-e District, an advanced area in ancient Tango with connections to major transport routes. The shrine's status as a revered local protector persisted through the Edo period, with additional legends linking it to figures such as {{ill|Prince Maroko|ja|麻呂子親王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Maroko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 当麻皇子}} and King Ōke and Kōke (future emperors).
=== Modern Period === The shrine was designated as a village shrine in 1873 and later promoted to Fusha in 1918. It underwent reconstruction after a fire in 1877 and subsequently absorbed two smaller local shrines, Atsue Shrine and Tokura Shrine. The shrine remains part of the [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]].
== festivals == The shrine is central to the {{ill|Kayadani Festival|ja|加悦谷祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kayadani Festival|1=A feast day of Kayadani|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q63795744}}, a joint event with Komushi Shrine, involving ritual dances and processions. Highlights include: * '''Taiko Drum Performances:''' Traditional drumming to honor the deities. * '''Sword Dances:''' Skilled displays by children and youth participants. * '''Processions and Offerings:''' Sacred objects are paraded and presented at both shrines.
== Cultural Properties == * '''Five-story Stone Pagoda''' (1982, Yosano Town-designated cultural property) * '''Fragments of Buddhist Sutras:''' Excavated from the precincts' sutra mound, including references to "Taira-no-Yasuji," a local historical figure. == See Also == * {{ill|Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)|ja|大虫神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)|qq=}} - A similarly named shrine in Echizen, Fukui Prefecture.
== References == * "Kaya Town History" Volumes 1 & 2, Kaya Town Office, 1974 & 2007. * "Shikinaisha Research Reports, Vol. 18," Kōgakkan University Press, 1984. * "Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places," Vol. 7, Heibonsha, 2000. * "Place names of Kyoto Prefecture," Heibonsha, 1981.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Omushi-jinja (Yosano)]][[ja:大虫神社 (与謝野町)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11438782]] Wikidata name: Omushi-jinja (Yosano) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Ōmushi Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:与謝野町 大虫神社8.jpg | location = Atsue, Yosano Town, Yosa District, Kyoto Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35.493417|N|135.117583|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = Ōnamuchi no Mikoto | shrine_Rank = Shikinaisha (Myōjin Taisha), Former Fu-sha | founded = Unknown | architecture = Gable style (Kirimitsukuri) | festivals = Last Sunday of April | rituals = Kayadani Festival (Last Saturday and Sunday of April) | prefecture= {{ill|Yosano|WD=Q1203259}} }}
'''Ōmushi Shrine''' (大虫神社, ''Ōmushi Jinja'') is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in Atsue, [[:en:Yosano, Kyoto|Yosano Town]], Kyoto Prefecture, Japan. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} (Myōjin Taisha) and a former {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社|1=Metropolitan Shrine|WD=Q134917326}}{{efn|lt=Fu-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. The shrine stands on an elevated plain to the east of the Kayadani region, a central area in the ancient {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後地方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tango Province|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 丹後国|1=Tango Province|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1054331}}, known for its historical importance and dense clusters of ancient Kofuns and archaeological sites.
== Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大己貴命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} - Primary deity * {{ill|Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja|少童命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ワタツミ|1=Watatsumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1474187}} - Enshrined following the 1881 merger of Atsue Shrine * {{ill|Ōta no Mikoto|ja|大田命|1=Ōta-no-Mikoto|2=ja|3=太田命|4=fr|5=大田命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōta no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōta-no-Mikoto|qq=}} - Enshrined following the merger of Tokura Shrine in 1881
== History == === Founding Myths === The shrine was once located on the mid-slopes of [[:en:Ōeyama (mountain range)|Mount Ōe]] at a site called Ikeganaru, along with the nearby {{ill|Komushi Shrine|ja|小虫神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komushi Shrine|qq=}}. It was popularly known as "Mushi no Miya" (Shrine of Insects). According to legend, when Ōnamuchi no Mikoto and {{ill|Nunakawahime|lt=Nunakawahime|lt=Numakawa-hime|ja|沼河姫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Numakawa-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 沼河比売}} resided in the area, a malevolent demon named Tsuchi Oni appeared, spreading illness. With the intervention of {{ill|Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ|1=Sukunabikona|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3275687}}, the demon was driven away, but the aftermath caused widespread affliction among humans and animals. To mitigate this, Sukunahikona no Mikoto became known as "Komushi" (Small Insect), purging illness from within, while Ōnamuchi no Mikoto was called "Ōmushi" (Large Insect), curing diseases externally. This narrative led to the worship of both as deities of healing.
=== Heian Period and Beyond === Historical records in the [[:en:Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]] note the shrine's elevation to the Rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, in 855 CE. The shrine is also listed in the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]] as a Myōjin Taisha and appears as "Shōichi-i Ōmushi Myōjin" in the surviving {{ill|Tango Province Shrine Register|ja|丹後国神名帳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tango Province Shrine Register|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. During the Kamakura period, it controlled over 18 hectares of shrine lands, remnants of which remain as local names such as "Gokuden" (Offering Fields) and "Tomyōden" (Lamp Fields).
In the early Muromachi period, the shrine was relocated to its current site, though the reasons remain unclear. The site lies in the historical Achi-e District, an advanced area in ancient Tango with connections to major transport routes. The shrine's status as a revered local protector persisted through the Edo period, with additional legends linking it to figures such as {{ill|Prince Maroko|ja|麻呂子親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Maroko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 当麻皇子}} and King Ōke and Kōke (future emperors).
=== Modern Period === The shrine was designated as a village shrine in 1873 and later promoted to Fusha in 1918. It underwent reconstruction after a fire in 1877 and subsequently absorbed two smaller local shrines, Atsue Shrine and Tokura Shrine. The shrine remains part of the [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]].
== festivals == The shrine is central to the {{ill|Kayadani Festival|ja|加悦谷祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kayadani Festival|1=A feast day of Kayadani|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q63795744}}, a joint event with Komushi Shrine, involving ritual dances and processions. Highlights include: * '''Taiko Drum Performances:''' Traditional drumming to honor the deities. * '''Sword Dances:''' Skilled displays by children and youth participants. * '''Processions and Offerings:''' Sacred objects are paraded and presented at both shrines.
== Cultural Properties == * '''Five-story Stone Pagoda''' (1982, Yosano Town-designated cultural property) * '''Fragments of Buddhist Sutras:''' Excavated from the precincts' sutra mound, including references to "Taira-no-Yasuji," a local historical figure. == See Also == * {{ill|Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)|ja|大虫神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)|qq=}} - A similarly named shrine in Echizen, Fukui Prefecture.
== References == * "Kaya Town History" Volumes 1 & 2, Kaya Town Office, 1974 & 2007. * "Shikinaisha Research Reports, Vol. 18," Kōgakkan University Press, 1984. * "Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places," Vol. 7, Heibonsha, 2000. * "Place names of Kyoto Prefecture," Heibonsha, 1981.
== External Links == * [https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/jinja/300401_53204/ Ōmushi Shrine - Kokugakuin Digital Museum]
{{citation needed|ja|大虫神社|version=39256620|comment=Imported full ja history}}
